titleEn: The Mu'allaqa of Imru' al-Qays

verses.sadr: قِفَا نَبْكِ مِنْ ذِكْرَى حَبِيبٍ وَمَنْزِلِ

verses.ajuz: فَهَلْ عِنْدَ رَسْمٍ دَارِسٍ مِنْ مُعَوَّلِ

translationEn: Stop, let us weep over the memory of a beloved and her dwelling at the edge of the sand dune between al-Dakhul and Hawmal. The traces at Tudih and al-Miqrat have not been erased, for the south and north winds have woven over them. You can see the droppings of gazelles in its courtyards and hollows, like scattered peppercorns. On the morning of parting, the day they loaded up, I stood by the acacia trees of the tribe, cracking colocynth in anguish. My companions halted their mounts beside me, saying: do not perish from grief, but bear it with dignity. My only cure is a flood of tears — but what solace is there at a faded trace? Just as you wept before for Umm al-Huwairith and her neighbor Umm al-Rabab at Ma'sal. When those two women rose, the scent of musk wafted from them like the morning breeze carrying the fragrance of cloves.
context: The opening of the most famous of the Mu'allaqat (Suspended Odes), considered the pinnacle of pre-Islamic Arabic poetry. The poet halts at the ruins of his beloved's abandoned campsite, a conventional opening (nasib) that Imru' al-Qays elevated to an art form.

titleEn: Description of the Horse

verses.ajuz: كَجُلْمُودِ صَخْرٍ حَطَّهُ السَّيْلُ مِنْ عَلِ

verses.sadr: كُمَيْتٍ يَزِلُّ اللِّبْدُ عَنْ حَالِ مَتْنِهِ
إِذَا مَا جَرَى شَأْوَيْنِ وَابْتَلَّ عِطْفُهُ

verses.ajuz: إِذَا جَاشَ فِيهِ حَمْيُهُ غَلْيُ مِرْجَلِ

translationEn: Charging, fleeing, advancing, retreating — all at once — like a boulder hurled down by a torrent from on high. A bay horse whose saddle-pad slips from the crest of his back, as a smooth rock deflects rainwater. He has the flanks of a gazelle, the legs of an ostrich, the loping stride of a wolf, and the gallop of a young fox. When he has run two laps and his sides grow wet, you would say it is the whistling of wind through a great tree. Champing at the bit, surging as though his seething, when his ardor boils, were a cauldron at full boil. He throws a light rider from his back and twists off the robes of the heavy, rough one.
context: This celebrated passage from the Mu'allaqa of Imru' al-Qays is one of the greatest descriptions of a horse in Arabic literature. The poet uses a cascade of animal comparisons to capture the horse's speed and power.

titleEn: Description of the Night

verses.sadr: أَلَا أَيُّهَا اللَّيْلُ الطَّوِيلُ أَلَا انْجَلِي
فَيَا لَكَ مِنْ لَيْلٍ كَأَنَّ نُجُومَهُ

translationEn: And a night like the waves of the sea let down its curtains upon me with all manner of sorrows to test me. I said to it when it stretched its back, trailed its haunches, and pressed down with its heavy chest: O long night, will you not lift to reveal the dawn — though the dawn is no better than you! What a night it is, as if its stars were tied with ropes of flax to solid boulders! As if the Pleiades were hung in their place by linen cords fastened to great stones.
context: This famous passage from the Mu'allaqa personifies the night as a great beast weighing down on the poet. The image of the night as a sea of waves became one of the most imitated motifs in Arabic literature.

titleEn: Description of Lightning and Rain
translationEn: Companion, do you see the lightning? I show you its flash, like the gleam of hands in a cloud crowned with more clouds. Its glow illuminates the sky, like the lamps of a monk who has tipped the oil over twisted wicks. I sat watching it with my companions between Darij and al-Udhayb, gazing from afar. By morning it was pouring water around Kutayfa, toppling the great trees of Kanahbal upon their trunks.
context: The closing section of the Mu'allaqa, where Imru' al-Qays delivers a magnificent description of a rainstorm and its aftermath. This passage demonstrates his mastery of nature poetry (wasf) that influenced all subsequent Arabic poets.

titleEn: Memory of the Beloved

verses.sadr: أَلَا عِمْ صَبَاحًا أَيُّهَا الطَّلَلُ البَالِي
verses.ajuz: وَهَلْ يَعِمَنْ مَنْ كَانَ فِي العُصُرِ الخَالِي

verses.sadr: أَلَا أَيُّهَا القَلْبُ الذِي بَرَّحَ الهَوَى
verses.ajuz: بِهِ حُبُّ خَوْدٍ ذَاتِ خَلْقٍ مُعَدَّلِ

verses.sadr: خَلِيلَيَّ مُرَّا بِي عَلَى أُمِّ جُنْدَبِ

verses.ajuz: مِنَ الدَّهْرِ تَنْفَعْنِي لَدَى أُمِّ جُنْدَبِ

translationEn: Greetings, O decayed ruin — but can one who belonged to ages past return a greeting? O this heart tormented by passion, the love of a graceful woman of perfect form! My two friends, take me past Umm Jundab so I may fulfill the longings of my anguished heart. For if you two would but look upon me for one moment in time, that would avail me greatly when I am near Umm Jundab.
context: A qasida attributed to Imru' al-Qays where he addresses the ruins of his beloved's campsite and asks his companions to take him past her dwelling. It showcases the conventional nasib (amatory prelude) of Jahili poetry.

titleEn: Will he slay me while my sword lies beside me

verses.ajuz: ... كجُلْمُودِ صَخْرٍ حَطَّهُ السَّيْلُ مِنْ عَلِ ...

verses.sadr: أرى أمّ عمرٍو دَمْعُهَا قد تحدّرَا
verses.ajuz: بُكَاءً على عَمرٍو، وَمَا كان أصْبَرَا
كُلىً ً من شَعِيبٍ ذاتِ سَحٍّ وَتَهْتانِ

verses.sadr: قفا نبكِ من ذِكرى حبيبٍ ومنزلِ

verses.ajuz: فهلْ عنْدَ رَسْمٍ دارِسٍ من مُعَوَّلِ
تَمَتَّعْتُ من لَهْوٍ بها غيرَ مُعْجَلِ

verses.sadr: ألا رُبّ خَصْمٍ فيكِ ألْوَى رَدَدتُه
فيا لكَ من لَيْلٍ كَأَنَّ نُجومَهُ
كِلانا إذا ما نالَ شيئًا أفاتَهُ
قِفا نبكِ، من ذِكرى حبيبٍ، ومنزلِ
فإن تزجراني يا بن عفَّانَ أنزجِر ْ

verses.ajuz: فهلْ عندَ رَسمٍ دارِسٍ مِنْ مُعَوَّلِ
عَلَى النَّحرِ حتّى بلّ دمعيَ مِحْمَلي

verses.sadr: ألا رُبّ يَومٍ لكَ مِنْهُنّ صَالِحٍ
على حينَ عاتبتُ المشيبَ على الصّبا
فتى كان أحيا من فتاةِ حييّةٍ
رمى الله في عيني بثنية بالقَذى
يا تيمَ تيمَ عَديٍ لا أبا لكمُو
تقولُ، وقد مال الغَبيطُ بنا معًا
verses.ajuz: عَقَرْتَ بَعِيري يا امرَأَ القَيس، فانزِلِ

verses.sadr: عهدي بها في الحي قد سربلت
يا ليت أمّ العمر كانت صاحبي
وقد تُخِذَتْ رجلي لدى جنب غرزها
إذَا ما بكَى من خَلْفها انْصَرَفَتْ لهُ

verses.ajuz: وَإن كنتِ قد أزمعتِ صَرْمي فأجملي

verses.sadr: وَإِنْ تَكُ قَدْ ساءَتكِ مني خَليقةٌ

verses.ajuz: تَمَتَّعْتُ من لَهْوٍ بها غيرَ مُعَجَّلِ

verses.sadr: إذَا مَا الثُّرَيَّا فِي السَّمَاءِ تَعَرَّضَتْ
فقالتْ: يَمينَ الله ما لكَ حيلَةٌ
verses.ajuz: وَما إنْ أرى عنكَ الغَوايةَ تَنْجلي
بنا بطنُ خَبْتٍ ذي حِقَافٍ عقَنقَلِ
نؤومُ الضحى لم تَنْتَطِقْ عن تفضّلِ

verses.sadr: لو أبصرتْ رهبانَ ديرٍ في جبلْ

verses.ajuz: إذا ما اسبكَرّت بين درْعٍ، ومِجْوَلِ

verses.sadr: ألا رُبّ خَصْمٍ فيكِ ألْوَى رَدَدتُه
ألا أيّها اللّيلُ الطويلُ ألا انْجَلِي
فيا لكَ من لَيْلٍ كَأَنَّ نُجومَهُ

verses.ajuz: إلى حسبٍ في قومه غير واضع

verses.sadr: فقُلتُ لَهُ لَمَّا عَوى: إنّ شأنَنا
verses.ajuz: قليلُ الغِنى إنْ كنتَ لَمّا تَمَوَّلِ

verses.sadr: كِلانا إذا ما نالَ شيئًا أفاتَهُ

verses.ajuz: كجُلمودِ صَخرٍ حطَّهُ السيلُ مِنْ عَلِ

verses.sadr: باتت تنوش الحوض نوشًا من علا
كُمَيتٍ يَزِلُّ اللّبْدُ عن حالِ مَتْنِه

verses.ajuz: إذا جاشَ فيه حميُهُ غَليُ مِرْجَلِ

verses.sadr: مِسَحٍّ إذا ما السَّابِحَاتُ على الوَنَى
كأنّ على الْمَتْنَيْنِ منهُ إذا انْتَحَى
فظلّ طُهاةُ اللَّحمِ من بَينِ مُنْضِجٍ

verses.ajuz: مَتَى ما تَرَقَّ العَينُ فيه تَسَفَّلِ

verses.sadr: يا حارِ لا أرمين منكم بداهية
verses.ajuz: لم يلقها سوقة قبلي ولا ملك
فَأَنْزَلَ منه العُصْمَ من كلّ منزِلِ

verses.sadr: وَتَيْمَاءَ لم يَتْرُكْ بها جِذْعَ نخلَةٍ

translationEn: Will he slay me while my Mashrafi sword lies by my side, and blue-tipped spears sharp as the fangs of ghouls? This vast collection of verses constitutes the great Mu'allaqa — a masterwork of over a hundred verses encompassing the traditional themes of pre-Islamic qasida: the abandoned campsite (nasib), the erotic encounter, the proud description of the horse, the desert crossing, and the celebrated storm scene. The poet halts at ruined traces of his beloved's camp between al-Dakhul and Hawmal, weeps at the memory of love, celebrates his amorous adventures with Fatima and Unaiza, describes his legendary horse with the flanks of a gazelle and legs of an ostrich, hunts with his companions through the wild terrain, and closes with a magnificent storm that floods the valleys and bends the great trees.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Mashrafi sword (from Masharif, a place of sword manufacture)
the edge/foot of the sand dune

context: The great Mu'allaqa of Imru' al-Qays, the most celebrated of the seven suspended odes of pre-Islamic Arabia. This scholarly edition (sharh) presents the poem with its extended tradition of additional verses. Imru' al-Qays is said to have composed and refined it throughout his turbulent life seeking to avenge his father's murder.

titleAr: ذهبتَ في الهجران في غير مذهبِ
titleEn: You departed in estrangement without cause

verses.sadr: ذهبتَ في الهجران في غير مذهبِ
verses.ajuz: ولم يك حقًا كلّ هذا التجنُّبِ

verses.sadr: خَليلَيَّ مُرّا بي على أُمِ جُنْدَبِ

verses.ajuz: من الدهرِ تَنفعْني لَدى أُمِّ جُندَبِ
وَوَجدْتُ بهَا طِيبًا، وَإنْ لم تَطَيَّبِ (٢)
وَلا ذَاتُ خَلقٍ، إنْ تأمّلتَ جَأنّبِ (٣)

verses.sadr: ألا ليتَ شِعرِي، كيفَ حادثُ وَصْلِها؟
أقَامَتْ على مَا بَيْنَنَا، مِنْ مَوَدّةٍ
فَإنْ تَنْأَ عَنْهَا، حِقْبَةً، لا تُلاقِها
تَبَصّرْ خَليلي هلْ تَرَى مِنْ ظَعائنٍ؟
وَلله، عَيْنَا، مَنْ رَأى مِنْ تَفَرُّقٍ

verses.ajuz: يَمُجُّ لِعاعَ البَقلِ في كُلِّ مَشرَبِ (٤)
وَماءُ الندى يجرِي على كلّ مِذْنَبِ (٦)
ومَثْناتَهُ في في رأسِ جِذْعٍ مُشذَّبِ (٥)

verses.sadr: إذا ما جَرَى شَأْوَينِ وَابْتَلّ عِطْفُهُ

verses.ajuz: بهِ عُرّة ٌ من طائفٍ، غَيرَ مُعْقِبِ (٧)

verses.sadr: فَأدْرَكَ لمْ يَجْهَدْ وَلمْ يَثنِ شَأوَهُ
تَرَى الفَأرَ في مُستَنقعِ القَاعِ لاحِبًا
verses.ajuz: على جَدَدِ الصّحرَاءِ من شدِّ مُلهِبِ
إذا نحنُ قُمْنَا عنْ شِوَاءٍ مُضَهَّبِ (٢)

translationEn: You departed in estrangement without just cause, and all this avoidance was not right. This long qasida, linked to the famous contest with the poet Alqama over Umm Jundab, combines the themes of longing, travel, and an extended description of the poet's horse. The poet addresses his two companions, asking them to pass by Umm Jundab's dwelling so he might satisfy the yearning of his tormented heart. He describes the departure of the tribe's caravans, his emotions at separation, and launches into a detailed description of his extraordinary horse — its slim flanks like a gazelle's, its ears that reveal noble breed, its eyes like a craftsman's mirror, its gleaming tail like a heavy palm cluster. The horse then hunts wild game, chasing bulls and cows, while the poet and his noble companions feast in their tent.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the best, the prize woman among her peers
the tethering place of a horse
the back of the skull (of a horse)

context: This long qasida is associated with the famous poetic contest between Imru' al-Qays and the poet Alqama ibn Abada, with Umm Jundab serving as judge. The contest was about which poet better described his horse, and its outcome was disputed by generations of critics. The poem encompasses themes of love, travel to Syria, and extended horse description.

titleEn: We are hurried toward a hidden decree
translationEn: We are being rushed toward a hidden decree, enchanted all the while by food and drink. We are like sparrows, flies, and worms — yet bolder than a pack of charging wolves. All noble qualities of character are what my ambition strives for, and by them I earn my keep. So ease your blame on me, my reproacher, for experience and noble lineage will sustain me. My roots have intertwined with those of the deep earth, yet death is stealing my youth — it will take my soul and my body, and lay me, a weary sick man, in the dust. Have I not worn out camels across every vast, shimmering desert? Have I not ridden into great armies to gain wide sustenance? I have wandered to the horizons until I was content simply to return. After al-Harith the King, son of 'Amr, and after the good Hujr of the tent-pillars, I hope for softness from the turns of fate — though fate never neglects even the hard mountain summits. I know that soon I shall be caught in the claws of talons and fangs, just as my father Hujr met his end, and my grandfather — and I do not forget the slain at al-Kulab.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the point/sharpness of a claw or fang

context: A reflective hikma poem in which Imru' al-Qays confronts the inevitability of death and the erosion of glory. He meditates on the passing of his father Hujr and grandfather, the futility of wandering, and his fatalistic acceptance of fate. It reflects the later, darker period of his life spent seeking Byzantine aid to avenge his father.

titleEn: O Hind, do not marry a weakling
translationEn: O Hind, do not marry a weakling who still wears his birth-amulet from childhood — a man with his anklets bound, limping as he hunts rabbits, holding the rabbit's knucklebone in his palm to ward off death lest he perish. I am not a fidgeter when seated, nor a clumsy oaf stumbling about. I am not a rheumatic weakling, easily led about when compelled against his will. She said: may my very soul ransom his youth and his flowing locks, before they whiten and fade — when it was black as coal, covering the lower back and spreading over his shoulders.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a limp or stiffness in the wrist or ankle
a fidgety person, one who cannot sit still

context: A short poem of fakhr (self-praise) and mockery of a rival suitor, where the poet advises a woman named Hind against marrying a weak, cowardly man and contrasts that type with his own virile youth. The beloved's reply at the end — praising his youth and black hair — adds a tender note of ghazal.

titleEn: Rain watered the watering-places and Lal'a
translationEn: A heavy Simaki (autumnal) rain watered the watering-places, the old well, and Lal'a, and the plateau of Ayhab. It passed over the two low grounds — the two flats of Unayza — then over Dhat al-Niqaa', it bore down and descended. When it slid down from the heights of Tumayya, the east wind dispersed it and it poured down.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: places of watering, troughs where camels drink
of the Simak star — autumnal rain season
low flat ground, a depression in terrain

context: A brief rain-description fragment (wasf al-matar), one of Imru' al-Qays's characteristic storm sequences. The poem maps the course of an autumnal downpour across named desert locations, many of which appear in the Mu'allaqa. Such topographical precision was a hallmark of his style.

titleAr: الخّيرُ ما طَلَعَتْ شَمسٌ ومَا غَرَبَتْ
titleEn: Good is sought as long as the sun rises

verses.sadr: الخّيرُ ما طَلَعَتْ شَمسٌ ومَا غَرَبَتْ
صُبّتْ عَلَيْهٍ ومَا تَنْصَبُّ من أَمَمٍ

translationEn: Good is pursued as long as the sun rises and sets — it is sought at the forelocks of horses, bound there by effort. Calamity was poured upon him without moderation — indeed, tribulation is poured upon the wretched in abundance.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the two wretched ones, the unlucky

context: A two-verse hikma fragment on the pursuit of virtue through horsemanship and the inevitability of suffering for the unfortunate. The phrase 'good is sought at the forelocks of horses' became proverbial in Arabic, expressing that nobility and reward are attained through effort and courage in battle.

titleEn: I witness the wild raid bearing me forward

verses.ajuz: صَقْعاءُ، لاحَ لَها في المَرْقَبِ الذِّيبُ (١)

verses.sadr: صُبَّتْ عَلَيْهِ وما تنْصَبُّ مِنْ أُمَمٍ
لا كالَّتي في هَواءِ الجَوِّ طّالِبَةً
verses.ajuz: ولا كَهَذَا الّذِي في الأرْضِ مَطلوبُ
مَا في اجْتِهَادٍ على الإصْرَارِ تَعْييبُ (٦)

verses.sadr: يَلوذُ بِالصَّخْرِ مِنْهَا بَعْدَ مَا فَتَرَتْ
والخَيرُ مَا طَلَعَتْ شَمسٌ وما غَرَبَتْ

translationEn: I have attended the wild, scattered raid, carried by a lean mare with gaunt cheeks, a swift racer. Her rider, when he rises to bridle her, looks like a rope-puller on a crooked pulley erected high. When onlookers see her approaching, they glimpse her white blaze and her white-stockinged legs. Her halting is aflame, her gallop decisive, her flesh taut in distinct bundles, her belly arched. Her foreleg swims through the air, her hind leg kicks fiercely, her eye flashes fire, her back is smooth and flat. The sweat pours from her, her sprint cascades downward, her gut is tucked up tight, her color jet-black. She is like a white-crowned eagle when the rainwater floods and she is in full power — she has spotted a wolf from her lookout. She sees his shape from the height of her perch, with rocky crags between her and where he stands. She swoops at him through the air with folded wings, the diving of the wind driving her downward. She falls upon him without moderation — for tribulation is poured upon the wretched. She grips like a heavy bucket whose handle and rope have failed at the worst moment. He slips out from beneath her, his side gashed. He retreats to the rocks after her power wanes — on the rock, showers of blood from both. She drags her breast across the ground, rubbing her tongue and jaw in the dust. Death missed him by a finger's width — yet no one saves himself unless it is written. He stays wedged in the rocks watching her, and watches for the night, for night is a curtain. Good is pursued as long as the sun rises and sets — sought at the forelocks of horses.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rubbing in dust, getting covered in earth

context: A celebrated qasida combining the themes of war-mare description and an eagle-wolf hunting scene. The eagle hunting a wolf is an extended metaphor of extraordinary vividness. The poem is also notable for the line 'tribulation is poured upon the wretched,' which became a proverb. The final verse closing with the pursuit of good through horses ties it to the warrior ethos.

titleAr: ألا يا لهف هِنْدٍ إثْرَ قَوْمٍ
titleEn: Alas for Hind's grief after those people

verses.sadr: ألا يا لهف هِنْدٍ إثْرَ قَوْمٍ

translationEn: Alas, the grief of Hind after those people who were the cure for every pain — yet they were not struck down! Their fortune protected them through the sacrifice of their father's sons, and the punishment fell instead on the unlucky ones. And 'Ilba' escaped them by a hair's breadth — had they caught him, the waterskins would have been emptied (in mourning).

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the cure, the healing — used as a metaphor for people
waterskins (singular: وطب) — emptying them signifies mourning

context: A brief elegy lamenting those killed in battle and relieved that others were spared. The poem reflects the tribal blood-feuds of pre-Islamic Arabia, where fortune determined who survived. The reference to Hind and 'Ilba' points to specific tribal conflicts associated with the poet's life and the wars surrounding his father's killing.

titleEn: Our neighbor, for calamities keep returning

verses.ajuz: ومَا هُوَ آتٍ في الزَّمانِ قَرِيبُ

translationEn: O our neighbor, calamities keep returning — and I shall remain as long as the mountain of 'Asib stands. O our neighbor, we are two strangers here, and every stranger is kin to another stranger. If you maintain our bond then kinship exists between us; but if you sever it, the stranger remains a stranger. O our neighbor, what has passed does not return, and what is yet to come in time is near. The true stranger is not he whose home is far away — but the one whom the earth has covered over is the true stranger.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: our female neighbor (one who is in proximity)
a well-known mountain (used as a symbol of permanence)
if you cut us off, sever connection
covered by earth — a euphemism for the dead

context: A moving hikma poem composed during the poet's exile and wanderings in Syria and Byzantium, addressing a neighbor woman from a position of loneliness. The final verse — that the true stranger is the one buried in the earth — became one of the most quoted lines in Arabic poetry, a profound meditation on mortality and estrangement.

titleAr: يَا بُؤسَ لِلقَلْبِ بَعْد اليَوْمِ ما آبَهْ
titleEn: Woe to the heart for what it returned to

verses.sadr: يَا بُؤسَ لِلقَلْبِ بَعْد اليَوْمِ ما آبَهْ
verses.ajuz: ذِكرَى حَبيبٍ ببعضِ الأرْضِ قد رَابهْ (٣)
والرَّأْسُ بَعدي رَأيتُ الشّيْبَ قد عابه

verses.sadr: عَمْدًا لأرْقُبَ مَا لِلجَوِّ مِنْ نَعَمٍ

translationEn: Woe to the heart for what it returned to — the memory of a beloved in some distant land that has troubled it. Sulaima said: I see you today dejected, and your hair — I see that grey has marred it since I last saw you. The locks have turned white after the darkness of the hair, like the fringe of a fine garment when you spread its hem. I climbed a high lookout where eagles nest at its summit, ascending it at dawn while the soul was fearful — deliberately to watch what livestock moved through the open sky: seeing what returned at evening and what stayed out in the wilds. I descended to a group of travelers with their camels hobbled, tousled-haired as though a thicket grew atop them. When we mounted, we drove them swiftly until we swept up the flocks and then their owners.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a full head of flowing hair
at dawn, when the sky brightens
animals or people who remain out, away from home

context: A poem of nostalgic ghazal opening with grief over remembered love and the sight of grey hair, followed by a raid narrative. Sulaima's observation that the poet has aged since she last saw him gives the poem a melancholy introspective quality unusual in Jahili poetry, reflecting perhaps the later exile period of Imru' al-Qays's life.

titleEn: I visited the tribe's dwelling at dawn
translationEn: I visited the dwellings of the tribe at al-Bakarat, then al-'Arima, then Burqat al-'Irat, and Ghawl, and Hullayit, and the slopes of Mun'ij, to 'Aqil and the well of al-Amarat. I sat with my cloak over my head, counting pebbles as my tears would not cease. Help me against worry and memories that crowd upon one afflicted with sorrow through the long complete nights, or measured against nights like them — days whose reckoning is bitter. I feel as though I and my pillion-rider and our saddlebag and cushion are atop a wild ass coming down to drink at the water-holes — braying over a pregnant she-ass in heat, like the hired man's four frisky animals, rough at herding the wives together, coarse, foul of mouth as a spear-tip, full of anger. They eat curly Ethiopian sorghum grass and drink the cold water in the chill mornings. He brought them to a little-frequented watering, fearing 'Amr the lurker in his hide. Their hooves grind the pebbles with firm, balanced feet — neither stumpy nor wasted. They let their tails hang as if their tips were the plaited loops of well-known swords. And a hard camel like planks of a bier I drove along a clear road like a striped robe. I left her after her fullness was a burden — she stretched forward on bent joints. And a white sword like a toy-wand, I wore down its edge and struck with it at the calf and knee.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a place name; also means dawn times

context: A complex qasida opening with a listing of abandoned tribal camping spots across the desert, followed by reflective grief and an extended simile comparing the poet's emotional state to a wild-ass braying in the desert. The poem transitions to a camel-ride description and ends with a sword image, showing the characteristic Jahili movement from love-longing to journey and combat.

titleEn: Your night at Athmad has grown long

verses.sadr: لَقُلْتُ، مِنَ القَوْلِ، ما لا يَزَا

translationEn: Your night at Athmad has grown long, and the carefree man sleeps while you cannot. He spent the night and his night was like the night of one with a painful eye-ailment and trachoma. This comes from news that reached me — I was told it by Abu al-Aswad. Had it come from another, and the wound of the tongue is like the wound of the hand — I would have said words that would continue to be recounted, fixed like the pole-star. By what bond of ours do you desire peace — about the blood of 'Amr spilled by Marthad? If you bury the disease, we shall not hide it; if you kindle war, we shall not sit idle. If you kill us, we shall kill you; if you seek blood, we shall seek it. How long has it been since we knew the thrusting of warriors, and the praise and glory and sovereignty? And the building of pavilions, and the filling of bowls, and the fire and its kindled wood! I have prepared for war a leaping mare, swift to the whip and the spur. She swims through the air and plunges on, and her full gallop is like the crackling of burning palm fronds. And a coat of mail tightly riveted, interlinked, that narrows when folded like a file. Its sleeves pour over a man like a torrent flooding flat ground. And a long spear like the rope of a pulley bucket, from the bare palm-tree's fiber. And a sword with grooves, its blow precise — when it strikes the bone, it does not bend.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: kohl, antimony powder — also a place name
the grooves along a sword blade
did not bend, did not deflect

context: A war poem (fakhr) in the mutaqarib meter, combining the sleeplessness of grief with a proud challenge to enemies and detailed descriptions of his mare, chainmail, spear, and sword. The context involves the blood feud over his father Hujr's death and the negotiations for revenge. The line 'if you kill us, we shall kill you' became a famous expression of tribal reciprocity.

titleAr: ألا أبْلِغْ بَني حُجْرِ بنِ عَمْرٍو؛
titleEn: Convey to Banu Hujr ibn Amr my message

verses.sadr: ألا أبْلِغْ بَني حُجْرِ بنِ عَمْرٍو؛

translationEn: Convey to the sons of Hujr ibn 'Amr, and convey to that sharp-edged tribe, that I am perishing in the land of a foreign people, far and distant from your dwellings. Had I perished in my own people's land, I would have said: death is right — there is no immortality. I am striving daily with the kingdom of Caesar, and how worthy of death it is to lead to this end! In the land of Syria, there is no close kinsman, no healer to support me or visit me in illness. Had I met those camels at Usays and Haqat when they came down to us watering, on young she-camels that remain continuously garlanded with their reins, never swerving from the road.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who heals; a comforter in illness
to prop up, support (a sick person)
garlanded, wearing collars or reins continuously

context: A ritha (elegy) composed by the poet seemingly in anticipation of his own death, during his final journey seeking Byzantine aid. He sends a message to his tribe acknowledging he is dying in Syria far from home. The resigned acceptance — 'death is right, there is no immortality' — only applies if one dies among one's own people, making exile death particularly bitter.

titleEn: Did you remind yourself of what will not return
translationEn: Did you remind yourself of what will never return? The remembrance has stirred up a heart already stricken with grief. I remembered Hind and her companions of the same age, and I woke resolved to turn away from her in farewell. I drank with Caesar in his kingdom, and he honored me and I rode the postal horses. When we crowded together on the road, I outpaced the royal outrider by a great distance.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: stricken with grief, weighed down by sorrow
girls of the same age, companions
I resolved, I made up my mind firmly
I shared wine with, I drank with as a companion
the postal relay horses of the imperial system

context: A brief autobiographical poem that moves from grief over remembered love to pride at having been honored by the Byzantine Emperor Justinian and riding the imperial postal system. It encapsulates the paradox of the poet's final years: honored at court yet heartbroken and dying far from home.

titleEn: How wondrous Zubdan became smooth flat ground

verses.sadr: لا يَفْقَهُ القَوْمُ فِيهِ كلَّ مَنطِقِهمْ

translationEn: How wondrous is Zubdan — it became smooth, hard flat ground by evening, though it had been of solid stacked boulders. The people cannot understand all their speech within it except as whispers — you would think the sound is being bounced back.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: smooth flat ground, an even plain

context: A two-verse fragment describing the remarkable acoustics of a place called Zubdan — once rocky and loud, now so smooth and echo-prone that voices are bounced back as whispers. This precise observation of natural phenomena reflects Imru' al-Qays's keen descriptive eye that made him the master of wasf in Arabic poetry.

titleEn: I see my camels, praise God, have grown heavy
translationEn: I see my camels — praise be to God — have grown so heavy by morning that they labor when they face uphill slopes. They have grazed in the pastures of both sons of Zuhayr, feasting on fresh herbage until their skins grew tight.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the grazing territory, range of a tribe
skins, hides — tight skin indicating fatness

context: A brief pastoral fragment expressing satisfaction at well-fed camels — a mark of wealth and successful grazing in pre-Islamic Arabia. The reference to the sons of Zuhayr locates the grazing in a specific tribal territory, suggesting diplomatic relations or safe passage through allied lands.

titleEn: I saddled the white camels and urged them on
translationEn: I saddled the white camels and urged them forward at dusk, saying: go to the best of Ma'add! Make for Sa'd ibn al-Dibbab, hasten toward Sa'd — keep to Sa'd. He is a proud chief whose house branches from Iyad, standing between the noblest of al-Nabit and the clan of Sard.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I urged them on, drove them forward
at the time of weakness — late night or dusk
a proud chief, a thoroughbred stallion
the great northern Arab tribal confederation

context: A praise poem (fakhr/madh) in which the poet dispatches his camels toward the generous chieftain Sa'd ibn al-Dibbab, a figure of tribal nobility. Such poems of seeking hospitality or intercession were common in Imru' al-Qays's exile poetry as he moved through tribal territories seeking allies to help him reclaim his father's kingdom.

titleEn: I drive away poems from me as one drives cattle
translationEn: I drive away the poems from me as a bold young man drives away locusts. But when they multiply and exhaust him, he selects from among them varied excellent ones. So I set aside their coral to one side, and take from their pearls only what is finest.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: locusts — a simile for the swarming poems
pearls — the finest, most valuable poems
what is considered excellent, the best quality

context: A remarkable meta-poetic fragment in which the poet reflects on his own creative process: poems swarm at him like locusts, he drives them away but selects only the finest — the pearls among the coral. This self-aware commentary on the craft of poetry is rare in Jahili verse and reveals Imru' al-Qays's consciousness of aesthetic quality.

titleAr: سَمَا لَكَ شوْقٌ بَعدَما كان أقصَرَا
titleEn: Longing rose in you after it had subsided

verses.sadr: سَمَا لَكَ شوْقٌ بَعدَما كان أقصَرَا

verses.ajuz: لدى جانبِ الأفلاجِ من جنبِ تيمُرَا (٢)

verses.sadr: أوِ المُكْرَعاتِ من نَخيلِ ابنِ يامِنٍ
حَمَتْهُ بَنو الرَّبْدَاءِ مِنَ آلِ يامنٍ
كأنّ دُمَى شَفْعٍ على ظَهْرِ مَرْمَرٍ
غَلِقنَ برَهنٍ من حَبيبٍ بهِ ادّعتْ
وَكانَ لهَا في سَالِفِ الدّهرِ خُلّة ٌ
إذا نَالَ مِنْها نَظَرَة ً رِيعَ قَلْبُهُ
فَلَمّا بَدَتْ حَوْرَانُ في الآلِ دونها

verses.ajuz: أخوا الجَهدِ لا يلوِى على من تَعَذّرَا (٤)

verses.sadr: ولَم يُنْسِني ما قَدْ لَقِيتُ ظَعَائِنًا
كأَثْلٍ من الأعرَاض من دون بَيشَةٍ
فدَعْ ذا وَسَلِّ الهمِّ عنكَ بجَسْرَة ٍ

verses.ajuz: ترَى عند مجْرَى الضَّفرِ هرًّا مُشجَّرًَا (٩)

verses.sadr: عَلَيها فَتَىً لم تحْمِلِ الأرضُ مِثْلَهُ
هُوَ المُنْزِلُ الآلافَ من جَوّ ناعِطٍ
verses.ajuz: بَني أسَدٍ حَزْنًا من الأرضِ أوْعرَا (٣)

verses.sadr: وَلوْ شاءَ كانَ الغزْوُ من أرض حِميَرٍ
بَكى صَاحِبي لمّا رأى الدَّرْبَ دُونه
فَقُلتُ لَهُ: لا تَبْكِ عَيْنُكَ إنّمَا

verses.ajuz: بَرِيدِ السُّرَى باللّيلِ من خيلِ بَرْبرَا (٨)
تَرَى الماءَ من أعْطَافِهِ قد تحدَّرَا (٩)
مَشَى الهَيْدَبى في دَفّه ثمّ فَرْفَرَا (١٠)
وَلابنُ جُرَيجٍ في قرَى حِمصَ أنكَرا
وَلا شَيءَ يَشفي منكِ يا بنَة َ عَفزَرَا (٢)

verses.sadr: من القاصِرَاتِ الطَّرْفِ لوْ دبّ مُحْوِلٍ
verses.ajuz: من الذَّرّ فَوْقَ الإتْبِ منها لأثّرَا (٣)

verses.sadr: لَهُ الوَيْلُ إنْ أمْسَى وَلا أُمُّ هَاشِمٍ
أرَى أُمَّ عَمرٍو دَمْعُهَا قَد تحدّرَا
verses.ajuz: بُكَاءً على عَمرٍو وَمَا كان أصْبَرَا

verses.sadr: إذا نحْنُ سِرْنَا خَمسَ عَشرَةَ لَيلَةً
إذا قُلْتُ هذا صَاحِبٌ قد رَضيتُهُ
ألا رُبّ يَوْمٍ صَالِحٍ قَد شهِدْتُهُ
verses.ajuz: بتَاذِفَ ذاتِ التَّلِّ من فَوْق طَرْطرَا (٩)

verses.sadr: وَلا مِثْلَ يَوْمٍ في قُذَارَانَ ظَلْلتُهُ
فَهَل أنا ماشٍ بَينَ شَرْطٍ وحَيَّةس،
verses.ajuz: وهَل أنا لاقٍ حَيَّ قيْسِ بْنِ شَمَّرَا (٣)

verses.sadr: تَبَصَّرْ خَليلي هَل تَرى ضَوْءَ بَارِقْ
verses.ajuz: يُضيء الدُّجَى باللَّيلِ عن سَرْوِ حِمْيرَا (٤)
وجَوًّا فَرَوَّى نَخْلَ قيْسِ بْن شَمَّرَا (٥)

verses.sadr: وعَمْرُو بْنُ دَرْماءَ الهُمامُ إذا غَدا
وكُنتُ إذا مَا خِفْتُ يومًا ظلامةً

translationEn: Longing rose in you after it had subsided, and Sulaima settled in the basin of Faww and Ar'ar. A woman of Kinana who departed while love for her remained in the heart, as neighbors of Ghassan and the settled tribe grew around her. My two eyes watched the tribe's departure as they loaded up by the side of al-Aflaj from the flank of Taymura. In the heat-shimmer as they clustered together, I compared them to gardens of dom palms or a tar-coated ship, or to the well-watered palms of Ibn Yamin below the white rocks near al-Mushaqqar — tall noble palms with dense boughs bearing red half-ripe clusters, guarded by Banu al-Rabda' of Yamin's clan with their swords until the palm settled and was laden. The poem continues through vivid descriptions of the beloved's adornments and scent, nostalgic memories of good days in Syria, a journey by camel to the Byzantine court, and a close of lightning-watching and rain.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the departure of the tribe, traveling caravans
heat-mirage, the shimmering of hot air
sheltered maidens, girls kept in seclusion
a close friend, a lover, a companion
women who lower their gaze modestly

context: One of Imru' al-Qays's longest and most complex qasidas, this poem blends love-longing for Sulaima, vivid memory of good days in al-Sham (Syria), a camel journey, and the journey to seek Caesar's aid. The descriptions of the beloved's quarter and her scents, and the lightning-watching scene, show his mastery of extended imagery. The mention of Baalbek and Homs anchors it in his Syrian exile.

titleAr: لَعَمْرُكَ ما قَلْبي إلى أهْلِهِ بِحُرْ
titleEn: By your life, my heart does not return freely to its people

verses.sadr: لَعَمْرُكَ ما قَلْبي إلى أهْلِهِ بِحُرْ

verses.ajuz: أحَبُّ إلَيْنَا من لَيَالٍ عَلى أُقُرْ (١)
وَلِيدًا وَهَلْ أفْنى شَبَابيَ غيرُ هِرْ (٢)

verses.sadr: إذا ذُقتَ فَاهَا قلتَ طَعمُ مُدَامَة ٍ

verses.ajuz: لدى جُؤذَرَينِ أوْ كبعض دمى هَكِرْ (٤)
نَيسمَ الصَّبَا جاءتْ برِيحٍ من القُطُرْ (٥)
من الخَصّ حتى أنزَلوها على يُسُرْ (٦)

verses.sadr: فلمّا استَطابوا صُبَّ في الصَّحن نصْفُهُ
verses.ajuz: وَشُجّتْ بماءٍ غير طَرْقٍ وَلا كَدِرْ (٧)

verses.sadr: بمَاءِ سَحَابٍ زَلّ عَنْ مَتنِ صَخرَة ٍ
verses.ajuz: إلى بطن أُخْرَى طيّبٍ ماؤها خَصِرْ (٨)

verses.sadr: لَعَمْرُكَ ما إنْ ضرّني وَسْطَ حِميَرٍ

verses.ajuz: وَلا نَأنَإٍ يَوْمَ الحِفاظِ وَلا حَصِرْ (١١)

verses.sadr: لَعَمرِي لَقَوْمٌ قد نَرَى أمسِ فيهِمُ

verses.ajuz: أحَبُّ الَينا مِنكَ فَا فَرَسٍ حَمِرْ (٤)
وَمن خالِهِ وَمِن يَزِيدَ وَمِن حُجُرْ (٥)

verses.sadr: سَمَاحَة َ ذَا وَبِرَّ ذَا وَوَفاءَ ذَا
verses.ajuz: وَنَائِلَ ذَا اذا صَحَا وَاذا سَكِرْ

translationEn: By your life, my heart does not return freely to its people, nor does it settle for one day to bring me tranquility. Time is but nights and seasons — it does not persist steadily on anything upright. Nights at Dhat al-Talh by Muhajjar are dearer to me than nights on the plateau of Uqur. I take the morning drink at Hirr's side, attending her as a youth — and who spent my youth except Hirr? When you taste her mouth you say: the taste of aged wine brought by merchants. The two of them are does from the gazelles of Tabala, beside two fawns, or like some idols of Hakr. When they rise, musk wafts from them — the morning breeze carrying the scent of incense-wood. Like merchants who carried a jar of wine up from the riverside settlement until they set it down on easy ground, and when they found it good they poured half into the bowl and mixed it with water neither muddy nor stale — water of cloud that slid from the back of a rock into another pool, pure and cold. Sa'd is no companion of a sinner nor a coward on the day of honor's defense — he is generous whether sober or drunk. Yesterday I saw people in whose midst were tethering-posts for foals and flocks of great wealth — dearer to me than folk in mountain fastnesses preyed upon by leopards. Sa'd entertains us and comes to our gathering with paired full wineskins and with slaughtered animals.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a jar of wine brought from abroad

context: A reflective ghazal and praise poem addressed to a generous companion named Sa'd, set against nostalgic memories of youth and wine-drinking with two beautiful women. The poem balances love-longing, philosophical resignation to time's passing, and warm praise of a loyal friend — themes characteristic of Imru' al-Qays's later exile poetry.

titleEn: Many an archer from Banu Tha'al

verses.ajuz: مَا لَهُ لاَ عُدَّ مِنْ نَفَرهْ (٥)

translationEn: Many an archer from Banu Tha'al, inserting his hands deep into his hunting blind, presenting a bow of naSham wood, not bending it too far over its string — the wild game came to him to drink, and he shifted to drawing from his left side. He shot at them in their shoulder-muscles by the edge of the pool or its deep center, with an arrow from his quiver flashing like glowing embers in their sparks — fletched with the feathers of a young eagle and honed on his whetstone. His arrows never fail to kill — may God not count him among his tribe's fighting men! He is given over entirely to hunting, with no other livelihood in his old age. And a close friend I parted from yet did not weep for his tracks. And a cousin for whom I left the clear water of the pool, reserving the clean from the murky for him. And the travelers' talk that day at such-and-such a place — a conversation brief yet precious. And a cousin whose loss has stricken me, like the light of the full moon in its early shining.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a hunting blind, a hide for an archer
a bow that curves (either crooked or beautiful)
a type of hard wood used for bows
the muscles of the shoulder/chest area
grows, increases — here: arrow that fails to kill
the early days of the month when the moon is brightest

context: A poem in the rare madadid meter combining a vivid description of an expert archer with wisdom reflections on friendship, kinship, and loss. The archer passage is famous for its precise technical knowledge of archery. The elegiac close mourning a cousin as bright as the full moon shows the characteristic Jahili movement from descriptive wasf to personal grief.

titleEn: The sons of 'Awf built a noble lineage
translationEn: The sons of 'Awf built a lineage of honor which the treacherous insiders ruined when they betrayed. They fulfilled their protective pledge to their neighbor, and the one they defended was not abandoned in his absence. They did not do what the clan of Hanzala did — indeed, those ones consulted badly! No Himyarite kept faith, and neither did 'Adas — nor does the rump of a wild ass care about its breeching strap. But 'Uwair kept his covenant faithfully — neither a blemish of one-eye nor short stature shamed him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the duty of protection, a pledge of safe escort
a covenant, a pledge of honor and protection

context: A tribal fakhr poem praising the loyalty of 'Uwair and the sons of 'Awf in keeping their covenants, contrasted with the treachery of other groups. The verse about the rump of a wild ass is a rough and earthy tribal insult. Such poems of honor and betrayal were central to the tribal political discourse of pre-Islamic Arabia.

titleEn: A steady downpour with gentle drizzle in it
translationEn: A steady downpour with gentle drizzle in it, covering the earth as it seeks out every hollow and pours. It reveals the land's affection when it clears briefly, then hides it again when the downpour thickens. You see the sand-lizard quick and skillful, bending its claw, not touching the wet ground. You see the trees in the first gush of the rain like heads with their veils removed. Then for a moment a heavy shower falls with trailing flanks, soft and pouring down. The east wind stirred it and then a burst of south wind drove into it and burst open. It poured in torrents until the breadth of Khaym, then Khafa', then Yusur could barely contain its flood. I rode out on a horse that carried me at the tip of its nostril — with tucked flanks, well-built and taut.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a sustained rain that pours steadily
fine gentle drizzle mixed with heavier rain
when it lightened and cleared briefly
the first rush of rain, the initial flood
a sudden burst or squall of rain

context: A celebrated rain-description poem (wasf al-matar) in the ramal meter. Imru' al-Qays's rain descriptions are among the finest in Arabic poetry, anticipating the great storm scene of the Mu'allaqa. This poem tracks the passage of a storm across named desert locations, describing its effect on animals and trees with the precise eye of a desert dweller.

titleAr: لَنِعمَ الفَتى تَعشُو إلى ضَوْءِ نَارِهِ
titleEn: How fine the young man whose fire one seeks in darkness

verses.sadr: لَنِعمَ الفَتى تَعشُو إلى ضَوْءِ نَارِهِ
verses.ajuz: طَريفُ بن مَلءٍ ليلةَ الجوع وَالخَصَرْ (٤)

translationEn: How fine the young man whose fire one gropes toward in the dark — Tarif ibn Mil' on a night of hunger and bitter cold! When the hump-backed old she-camel comes in at evening seeking shelter among the trees from the voices of those who drive her off.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to grope toward a light in darkness, seeking it
an old she-camel of nine years with a large hump
to seek shelter, to dodge from side to side
those who drive camels away (by making 'biss' sounds)

context: A brief praise poem celebrating the generosity of Tarif ibn Mil', whose fire of hospitality is visible and sought by travelers on cold, hungry nights. The image of the she-camel seeking shelter among the trees adds a vivid naturalistic touch. Hospitality poetry of this kind was among the most socially important genres in pre-Islamic Arabia.

titleEn: O Harir Amr, I am as though intoxicated

verses.ajuz: وَلم يُفشُ منّا لَدى البَيتِ سِرْ (٧)
ءِ رُكّبنَ في يَوْمِ رِيحٍ وَصِرْ (١)

translationEn: O Harir 'Amr, I am as though intoxicated — and what a man plots against himself comes to pass. By your father, O daughter of al-'Amiri, the people shall not claim I fled! Tamim ibn Murr and their allies, and Kinda all around me — steadfast. When they mounted and donned armor, the earth shook and the day was bitterly cold. Will you leave the tribe at evening or at dawn, and what harm to you if you wait? Are their tents of markh-wood or of 'Usher — or does the heart slide after them by the road? Among those who stayed behind from the tribe is Hirr — or have the departing ones carried her away? Hirr hunts men's hearts — and Ibn 'Amr Hujr escaped her! She shot me with an arrow that struck my heart on the morning of departure, and I could not retaliate. My tears poured like scattered pearls or the flowing sheen of dripping gems. She walks like one intoxicated, thrown down by the exhaustion of the sand dune — soft-fleshed, tender, supple, like the young shoot of a ban tree just split open. Slow to rise, sparse of speech, she flashes teeth of cool, bright whiteness. Her cool teeth hold the taste of wine, rainwater, wild lavender, and fragrant wood combined — infused when the singing bird fills the pre-dawn air with melody. I spent the full night struggling, my heart trembling with awe. When I drew close, I mounted her — letting one garment fall and dragging another. No jealous watcher saw us and no secret of ours was revealed by the household. Her words troubled me: 'O you wretch, you've added harm upon harm!' Then I describe my fast hunting mare in forty extraordinary verses, body part by body part.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a woman with soft, lustrous white skin
a supple young shoot of a ban tree
the edges of bright teeth, their gleam
the fetlock hairs of a horse

context: One of Imru' al-Qays's longest surviving poems, this masterwork in the mutaqarib meter blends a love encounter with a woman named Hirr, tribal war-boasting, a night-vigil of longing, a love scene, a hunting dog passage, and an extraordinarily detailed forty-verse anatomical description of his mare — each body part compared to a different natural object. The horse-description passage is considered the most technically detailed in Arabic poetry.

titleAr: مَنَعتَ اللّيثَ من أكلِ ابنِ حُجْرِ
titleEn: You prevented the lion from devouring Ibn Hujr

verses.sadr: مَنَعتَ اللّيثَ من أكلِ ابنِ حُجْرِ

translationEn: You held back the lion from consuming Ibn Hujr, and the lion nearly destroyed Ibn Hujr. You held it back, and so you are one of favor and grace toward me, O son of al-Dibbab, as all know. I shall thank you for the defense you mounted on my behalf — and nothing repays you from me except my gratitude. For no neighbor is more reliable a protector than you, and your aid to one who stands alone is the noblest of aids.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the lion — metaphor for a powerful enemy or danger
to destroy, to cause the death of
the granting of a favor, beneficence
the lone one, the solitary — one without support

context: A gratitude poem addressed to Ibn al-Dibbab (likely Sa'd ibn al-Dibbab mentioned in an earlier poem) who protected the poet from a powerful enemy. The 'lion' is a metaphor for whoever threatened the poet's life during his exile. The poem is remarkable for its frank acknowledgment that gratitude is all he can offer — a nobleman's honest admission.

titleEn: Convey to Banu Zayd what I have met from them

verses.sadr: وَأبْلِغْ وَلا تَتْرُكْ بَني ابنَةِ مِنْقَرٍ

translationEn: Convey to Banu Zayd what I have experienced from them, and convey to Banu Lubna and convey to Tumadira. And convey — do not fail — to the sons of Ibn Minqar: I shall leave them without backbone, for I shall strip Nabira of all support. O Hanzal! Had you been noble men you would have been patient and protective — but a Tamimi is never found to be steadfast.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a woman's name — possibly a tribal name or a proper noun
I shall strip them of their backbone/support (from فَقَار: vertebra)
a person or tribe — subject of defiance
colocynth (bitter plant) — a tribal nickname meaning bitterness
a man from Banu Tamim — here used as a type of unreliable person

context: A brief fakhr/hija' (boasting/invective) poem directed at enemy tribes who betrayed the poet's trust. The anaphoric 'convey' (أبلغ) repeated at the start of each verse is a rhetorical device of emphatic warning. The final verse insulting the Tamimis became a point of tribal controversy in Arabic literary history.

titleAr: أمَاوِيَّ! هَلْ لي عِنْدَكُم من مُعرَّسِ
titleEn: O Mawiyya, is there a stopping place with you for me

verses.sadr: أمَاوِيَّ! هَلْ لي عِنْدَكُم من مُعرَّسِ

verses.ajuz: كِلابُ ابنِ مُرّ أوْ كلابُ ابنِ سِنْبِسِ (١)

verses.sadr: وَأيقَنَ إنْ لا قَيْنَهُ أنّ يَوْمَهُ
verses.ajuz: بذِي الرَّمثِ إنْ ماوَتْنهُ يوْمُ أنفُسِ (٤)

translationEn: O Mawiyya, is there a stopping place with your people for me, or do you choose severance and leaving me to despair of union? Make it clear to me — for a clean decision is a relief from the doubt of tangled indecision. I feel as though I and my saddle are atop a five-year-old grey wild ass at Shurba or wandering around 'Irnan, wary and alert. He grazed briefly then drove his hooves into the ground, stirring up the dust from his sleeping place and his shelter. He scoops the sand and scatters it, raising it like a digger in the midday heat for five days. He slept on a dark cheek and shoulder, his sleeping position like a bound prisoner. He slept against a tamarisk bush of the dune as if — when a brief shower dampened it — it were the tent of a new bridegroom. At sunrise, the dogs of Ibn Murr or Ibn Sinbis surprised him, lean and grey-eyed, their eyes from intensity and urging like the flowers of a 'Adras plant. He fled from them trailing dust on his back, like a firebrand on the high ground and hillocks. He realized, if they did not catch him, that his day at Dhu al-Ramth — if they cornered him — would be the day of his soul. They caught him and seized his leg and hamstring, as boys tear the robe of a holy man. Then they retired into the shade of the ghada trees and left him like a proud white stallion basking in the sun.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a resting place, a halt on a journey
a decisive break, a firm final decision
a wild ass with a white belly-band
a five-year-old horse or wild ass at full maturity
bound hand and foot, trussed up

context: A qasida opening with a poignant plea to the beloved Mawiyya for a clear answer — union or separation. The poet then launches into a celebrated extended simile comparing himself to a wild ass tracked by hunting dogs across the desert landscape. The simile occupies most of the poem and is one of the finest hunt narratives in pre-Islamic Arabic poetry, ending with the noble animal at bay in the sunlight.

titleEn: Pause at the old campsite of As'as

verses.sadr: فلوْ أنّ أهلَ الدّارِ فيها كَعَهْدِنَا

verses.ajuz: من اللّيلِ إلّا أنْ أكبَّ فَأنْعَسَا

verses.sadr: وَيَا رُبّ يَوْمٍ قَدْ أرُوحُ مُرَجَّلًا
يَرُعنَ إلى صَوْتي إذا مَا سَمِعْنَهُ
verses.ajuz: كمَا تَرْعوِي عِيطٌ إلى صَوْتِ أعيَسَا (٢)

verses.sadr: أرَاهُنّ لا يُحْبِبنَ مَن قَلّ مَالُهُ
verses.ajuz: وَلا مَنْ رَأينَ الشَّيبَ فيهِ وَقَوّسَا

verses.sadr: وَمَا خِفْتُ تَبرِيحَ الحَياةِ كما أرَى

verses.ajuz: فَيا لكِ من نُعمَى تحَوّلنَ أبْؤسا

verses.sadr: لَقد طَمَحَ الطَّمّاحُ من بُعد أرْضِهِ
ألا إنّ بَعدَ العُدْمِ للمَرْءِ قِنْوَة ً

translationEn: Pause at the old campsite at As'as — I call out as if speaking to a mute. Had the people of the dwelling still been there as in our time, I would have found a midday resting place and a night-halt with them. Do not deny me — I am indeed the same man from the nights when the tribe settled at Ghawl and al-Al'as. If you see me not closing my eyes for a moment of the night except when I nod off and slump down — my old illness has returned and crept on me before dawn. I fear my illness may turn back on me and strike me down again. Many a distressed man I have charged behind in battle and thrust horsemen away from him until he could breathe. And many a day I rode out groomed and handsome, beloved by the white-skinned young women, their breasts still rounded. They start at my voice when they hear it, as the tall she-camels start at the sound of a white camel. I see them: they do not love a man of little wealth, nor one whom they see grey-haired and bent. I have never feared the anguish of life as I do now — my arms grow too weak to rise and dress myself. Would that it were a soul that dies all at once — but it is a soul that falls away piece by piece. I have been given a bleeding wound in place of health — what blessings have turned to miseries! Al-Tammah aimed from a far land to clothe me with his disease as a garment. Yet after poverty there is for a man wealth again, and after grey hair a long life and clothing.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the old campsite, the abandoned dwelling
dying piece by piece, soul falling away gradually
the Ambitious One — believed to be a Byzantine who poisoned him
clothing, good covering — a symbol of prosperity

context: This deeply personal ritha-like poem is believed to have been composed during Imru' al-Qays's final illness in Anatolia, possibly from the poisoned garment allegedly sent by the Byzantine Emperor. The line 'it is a soul that falls away piece by piece' is a harrowing image of slow dying. The poem oscillates between memories of past vitality — battles, women, groomed horseback rides — and the bitter reality of physical collapse.

titleEn: What dead creature rises through its death

verses.ajuz: رَوّى بها من مُحولِ الأرْضِ أيْبَاسَا

verses.sadr: مَا القَاطِعاتُ لأرضٍ لا أنِيسَ بهَا
تِلكَ المَنَايَا فَمَا يُبْقِينَ مِنْ أحدٍ
مَا السّابِقَاتُ سِرَاعَ الطَّيرِ في مَهَلٍ
تِلكَ الجِيادُ عَلَيها القَوْمُ قد سبحوا
مضا القَاطعَاتُ لأرْضِ الجَوّ في طَلَقٍ

verses.ajuz: دُونَ السّمَاءِ وَلم تَرْفَعْ لَه رَاسَا

verses.sadr: مَا الحاكمُونَ بلا سَمْعٍ وَلا بَصَرٍ

translationEn: What dead creature rises through its death — toothless, having grown neither tooth nor molar? That is the grain of barley, watered in its ears, which after long waiting brought forth heaps. What are the black and white ones whose names are one — that people cannot touch? Those are the clouds when the All-Merciful sends them, watering with them the parched droughts of the earth. What are the things whose vessels are laden with awe, cutting across long distances by travel and struggling ropes? Those are the stars when their rising times approach — I liken them in the darkness of the night to firebrands. What are those that cut across a land with no companion in it, coming swift and never returning reversed? Those are the winds when their gales blow — their trailing edges are enough as a sweeper of dust. What are those that strike openly and publicly, fiercer than an army full of boldness? Those are the Fates — they spare no one, sweeping up the foolish and leaving none of the clever. What are those that outrun the swift birds at leisure, that do not complain even if you bridled them with a saw? Those are the horses whose riders have swum upon them — they were on the morning of alarm like saddle-cloths on their mounts. What are those that cut through the sky-land in full gallop before dawn, traveling with no map of paper? Those are ambitions that leave a young man as a king below the sky, yet never raised a head for him. What are the judges with no hearing, no sight, and no eloquent tongue to amaze people? Those are the scales — the All-Merciful sent them down, the Lord of Creation, as a measure between people.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a year of drought, a year without rain
firebrands, live coals taken from a fire
the scales of justice — a concept of divine measure

context: This extraordinary poem is a munadhara (debate/riddle poem) between Imru' al-Qays and the poet 'Abid ibn al-Abras, composed in a distinctive question-and-answer (ما...تلك) format. Each verse-pair poses a riddle in verse one, and the other poet answers it in the next. The themes — barley, clouds, stars, winds, death, horses, ambitions, and scales of justice — are among the deepest meditations on the natural and moral order in pre-Islamic poetry. The closing reference to divine scales suggests possible early Islamic interpolation or the poet's exposure to monotheistic ideas.

titleEn: Whose Is This Ancient Ruin?
translationEn: Whose is this ancient ruin whose signs have aged across the passing of long epochs? If you see me afflicted with sores, I am like one wounded by gout. The plague has clothed me in a garment that appears worn though it has never been worn. You can see the traces of the pox in his skin, like the imprinting of rings in silk.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: remnants, traces of a ruined camp
one wounded, one whose foot is ulcerated

context: A short poem in which Imru' al-Qays describes the physical devastation of an illness — believed by some commentators to be the pox that afflicted him near the end of his life. The opening atlal (ruined campsite) motif is compressed into a single verse before giving way to an unflinching self-portrait of bodily suffering, unusual in Jahili poetry.

titleAr: أمِنْ ذِكرِ سلمَى أنْ نأتْكَ تَنوصُ
titleEn: Does the Memory of Salma Drive You Restlessly Away?

verses.sadr: أمِنْ ذِكرِ سلمَى أنْ نأتْكَ تَنوصُ
تَظَاهَرَ فِيهَا النِّيُّ لا هيَ بَكْرَةٌ
verses.ajuz: وَلا ذاتُ ضِغنٍ في الزِّمامِ قَمُوصُ

verses.sadr: تَصَيّفَهَا حَتى إذا لمْ يَسُغْ لهَا

translationEn: Is it the memory of Salma that drives you restlessly, so that you fall short of reaching her or press forward? How many a vast wasteland and open plain lie between you and her, and how much barren earth and thieves! She appeared to us one day beside 'Unayza, and her departure was imminent — a young camel about to travel. With black, thick-hanging braids and bright teeth that she polishes and cleans. The growth of her hair is like dark silk, its color like the thorns of the sant-tree — sweet and gleaming. Will a powerful, compact she-camel not divert your sorrow — compact-boned and sturdy? She has built up fat — no young filly, nor one that pulls angrily at the rein. Ever returning, ever eager, her pace unchallenged when the night-travelers' pace is said to be swift. It is as if I and my saddle and saddle-bag and cushion ride atop a great male ostrich beside whose nest in the sandy bend lie eggs tightly packed. When he strides back to the nest he drives her off, and she flees him in fear. Is it that, or a dark onager herding his mares, heavy with foals bursting with the weight of their young? His belly is drawn up by the tautness of his constant running, the flanks lean and rising to the back — hollow. His back and spine are like quivers with a shining strip running between them. There is a scar from a blow on his cheek, and his withers are galled by biting. They graze on grass shoots and tender herbs of the open plain that have recovered after grazing, fresh and green. They shed loose tufts of moulted hair that fly like a dark silk cloak scattered by wind and palm-frond. He kept them through the summer until the sparse vegetation and stubble of the high plateau could no longer suffice them. They vied with each other for the little moisture, were it not for locusts fallen prostrate in rows. He drove them with a neigh, and she turned toward him — long-pasterns, fleet of foot. He brought them in the final hours of night to a watering place, lush green pools whose water glistened. They drank in gulps, fearful, their kidneys and inner flanks trembling. He sent them out in the evening ascending the high ground — lean-bellied, massive as a boy's spinning top. A foal was left behind their rear ends; another lay slain in their path. He sent them out with his back molars flashing — a worn veteran stallion, lean-flanked, tightly-made.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: horse or camel in its fifth year, a veteran

context: A long polythematic qasida opening with the atlal and the memory of Salma, transitioning through a description of the she-camel mount, then extending into a celebrated passage describing a wild onager among his mares — one of the finest examples of wasf al-wahsh (description of wild animals) in Jahili poetry.

titleEn: Help Me Watch This Flashing Lightning

verses.sadr: فأضْحَى يَسُحُّ المَاءَ من كلّ فِيقةٍ
فَأُسْقي بهِ أُخْتي ضَعِيفَةَ إذْ نَأتْ
أرَى المرءَ ذا الاذوَادِ يُصبْحُ مُحْرِضًا
كأنّ الفَتى لم يَغْنَ في النّاسِ ساعَةً

translationEn: Help me watch this lightning I see flashing, illuminating a dark cloud mass upon white mountain peaks. At times its gleam subsides, and at times it surges like the lurching of a broken, crushed bird. From it emerge flashes like hands receiving the lots of victory from the one who casts them. I sat watching it with my companions between Darij and the high grounds of Yathlath and al-'Arid. It struck two plains and their flanks flowed, then the valley of al-Badi', then it headed toward the fertile land. Broad lands and fertile earth — channels of rain across a wide open plain. It began at dawn to pour its water from every cloud-breast, sweeping the mists across white flat expanses. May it water my sister Du'ayfa when she is far away, and when the place of visitation is distant save for poetry. From a watchtower sharp as a spearpoint I looked out, turning my gaze over a wide open space. I remained there, and with me my dark horse with his saddle-cloth, as if I were treating a broken wing. When sunset had hidden the sun from me, I descended to him standing on the low ground. His sleek cheek rivals the tip of a spear, like the blade of a hardened, tempered lance. I pressed him down with the reins when I mounted, but he raised a gaze neither listless nor strained. I ride out at dawn while birds still sit in their nests — on a bare-skinned horse with powerful forelegs, quick to seize. He has the short, lean flanks of a greyhound and the legs of an ostrich, like a stallion white camel pressing on to bite. He recovers on his two legs after exhaustion, as spring wells recover after being stirred. I startled a herd with clean hides, as a wolf startles a flock by night. He took them three at a time, then two, then four, leaving one behind in the channel of the torrent-track. He returned without disappointment or sluggishness, leaving water after water in floods. A wild donkey like the summit of a high mountain in glory and peak — I startled it with a horse that presses through the midday heat, ever rising. I see the man of many camels waking emaciated, like a sick young camel left behind in the camp. It is as if the youth never flourished among men for a single hour once his jaw-bones clash in the rattle of death.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a spring or well seeping through rock
the death-rattle, the moment of dying

context: A long polythematic qasida opening with a description of lightning — one of the classic themes of Jahili poetry — before transitioning to a hunting scene with the celebrated horse. The final verses shift to a somber reflection on mortality and the fate of men, typical of Imru' al-Qays's capacity to move from exuberant description to philosophical melancholy.

titleAr: جَزِعتُ ولم أجزَعْ مِنَ البَينِ مَجْزَعا
titleEn: I Grieved at Parting, Yet Did Not Truly Grieve

verses.sadr: جَزِعتُ ولم أجزَعْ مِنَ البَينِ مَجْزَعا
وَمِنْهُنَّ: سوْقي الخَوْدَ قَد بَلّها النَّدى

verses.ajuz: سِوَاكَ، وَلَكِنْ لم نَجِدْ لَكَ مَدْفَعا
قَتِيلانِ لم يَعْلَمْ لَنا النّاسُ مَصْرَعا

translationEn: I grieved at parting, yet did not truly grieve — and I consoled a heart captivated by full-breasted maidens. I became one who has bid farewell to youth, yet I keep watch over four joys of life. Among them: my word to the drinking companions, 'be gentle,' as they attend to one weeping from wine brimming over. Among them: the gallop of horses thrusting with their lances, racing toward an unsuspecting herd before it is startled. Among them: the swift driving of white camels through the encompassing night, heading for an unknown wasteland. Emerging from desert toward a village, renewing a bond or drawing near a coveted aim. Among them: my guiding of the tender girl dewed with night-moisture, watching over her suckling babe adorned with amulets. My transgression weighs heavily on her and her crying pains her, so she turns her neck to keep him from weeping aloud. I sent to her while the stars were rising, fearful that she might rise and be heard. She came with short, fearful steps through the night, her two sides pressing against four swelling breasts. They urged her along with the gait of one drunk with sleep, drowsiness flowing through her mind, cutting her. She said — when I had stripped her of her garments — as if you had startled a kohl-eyed, long-necked gazelle: 'By your father's honor, had any other messenger come to us, but we found no way to refuse you.' We spent the night while the wild beasts kept away from us, as if we were two slain lovers whose falling place no one knew. She drew aside from what lay between us — the sword — and drew the fine cross-striped silk close over me. When a tremor of startled fear seized her, she clutched the shoulder of a bold man undaunted by terror.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one drunk or suffering from loss of blood

context: A reflective qasida in which the poet declares farewell to youth while cataloguing four remaining pleasures of manly life: wine, the hunt, night travel, and love. The poem culminates in one of the most vivid nocturnal love episodes in Jahili poetry, describing a secret tryst with a nursing mother who comes fearfully through the night.

titleAr: لَعَمري لَقَدْ بَانَتْ بحَاجَةِ ذِي الهَوَى
titleEn: By My Life, She Departed with the Longing of a Lover

verses.sadr: لَعَمري لَقَدْ بَانَتْ بحَاجَةِ ذِي الهَوَى

verses.ajuz: إلى اللُّخِّ مَرْأى مِنْ سُعَادَ ومَسْمَعَا

verses.sadr: مَتَى تَرَ دَارًا مِنْ سُعَادَ تَقِفْ بِهَا

translationEn: By my life, Su'ad departed with the need of a passionate lover, and she alarmed with parting the one already alarmed. The garden-plots around Mukhattath as far as al-Lukh were filled with the sight and sound of Su'ad. Whenever you see a dwelling that belonged to Su'ad you stop there, and your eyes draw tears until they weep.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who is terrified, one alarmed
to draw out, to cause to flow

context: A brief fragment preserved in the scholarly sharh tradition, evoking the departure of Su'ad — a beloved whose name also appears in the famous ode of Ka'b ibn Zuhayr. These three verses display the concentrated emotional power typical of Imru' al-Qays's shorter pieces: the lover is paralyzed by association, unable to pass her former dwelling without weeping.

titleEn: He Dwelt at the Oasis Inside Busra
translationEn: He dwelt at the oasis inside Busra — the father of orphans and the lean, the dependent. Who now will protect the refugee when he calls, and who will bear the burden of the weak and lonely?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a palm oasis, a settlement among palms
the dependent, the burden, the helpless one
the refugee seeking protection, the guest
the weak or isolated ones seeking companionship
the lean, the emaciated, the poor

context: A two-verse elegy or lament for a deceased protector — the 'father of orphans' who sheltered dependents in the region of Busra (in the Hawran). The poem poses the rhetorical question that defines the ideal of Jahili chieftainship: who will now fulfill the duty of protection and hospitality?

titleAr: ألا عِمْ صَبَاحًا أيّهَا الرَّبْعُ وَانْطِقِ
titleEn: Good Morning, O Ruined Campsite — Speak!

verses.sadr: ألا عِمْ صَبَاحًا أيّهَا الرَّبْعُ وَانْطِقِ
verses.ajuz: وَحدِّثْ حديثَ الركبِ إن شئتَ وَاصْدقِ
تُنيفُ بعَذقٍ من غِرَاسِ ابن مُعنِقِ
تعفّي بذَيلِ الدِّرْعِ إذا جئتُ مَوْدِقي
ترَى التُّرْبَ منهُ لاصِقًا كلَّ مَلصَقِ
إلى غُصْنِ بَانٍ نَاصِرٍ لم يُحرَّقِ

verses.sadr: كَأنّ غُلامي إذْ عَلا حَالَ مَتْنِهِ
verses.ajuz: عَلى ظَهْرِ بَازٍ في السّماءِ مُحَلِّقِ

verses.sadr: فَقُلنَا: ألا قَد كانَ صَيْدٌ لِقَانِصٍ،

translationEn: Good morning, O ruined campsite — speak! Tell the tale of the caravan if you will, and tell it truly. Tell how their baggage-camels set out by night like date palms from a valley-garden, untrimmed. They made litters and mounted riding-seats and draped them with the figured weave of Iraqi fabric. Above the litters were gazelle-fawns and young deer, perfumed with potent musk and lily. I followed them with my gaze until the sand-dunes covered in tamarisk and thorny shrubs came between us. Following a tribe heading deliberately for a distant watering place, settling at al-'Aqiq or the mountain pass of Mutriq. I consoled myself when they departed with a powerful she-camel, steadfast as a Jewish architect's building, fleet and swift. When urged on she was found rushing forward, surging like a laden palm bunch from the plantation of Ibn Mu'niq. She moves in the evening with the gait of a distant cloud following a drifting cloud that scatters in all directions. As if a cat dragged by a leash were pulling her back on every road and narrow pass she took. It is as if I and my saddle and saddle-bag and cushion are mounted on an ostrich with huge feathers, a great male. Roaming from land to distant land, recalling the summer season around cracked eggs. Roving across the horizons of the lands going west, tossed by the east wind at every gust. And many a tent in whose inner rooms musk wafts — far from harm, never filtered and poured out. I entered upon a white-complexioned woman, full-limbed, trailing her dress-hem when I came to my place of calling. While the stars had come to rest at the center of the sky, halted as browsing oryxes stand still grazing. How often I set out before the sneeze of dawn on a powerful horse, thick-flanked and broad at the girth. We sent out a scout before that, low to the ground, creeping like a wolf of the ghada-trees, moving under cover and staying alert. He crouched like a fawn raising only his head while the rest of him blended with the powdered earth. Then he came forward in secret, his belly scraping the earth, the dust clinging to his every part. He said: 'Behold — a herd of wild bulls, a herd of onagers, and a line of ostriches grazing scattered.' We rose taking up the loose reins without tying to any supple bending branch. We worked on the horse until our lad mounted his back — tall and arching like a polished rib. It was as if my lad when he mounted the crest of his back rode on a soaring hawk circling the sky. It spotted a hare and plunged after it, diving headlong and fixing it with a flashing, darting gaze. I said to the lad: 'Angle him down, do not strain him, or he will toss you from the top of his rump and you will slip.' They fled like strung beads alternating in color around the neck of the lad in his embroidered collar. He caught them — with the rein half held — like an evening shower, dust-colored, dropping and dripping. He hunted a wild ass, a bull, and a ruddy one in continuous pursuit — never exerting himself to break a sweat. My lad remained laying the spear beside each oryx cow or striped, long-legged wild ass. He stood tall as they blooded him — standing like the noble Persian warrior belted for war. We said: 'Here is game fit for a hunter! So pass to us every embroidered garment you have!' My companions spent the day roasting in comfort, lining a cave with packed meat seasoned with fat. We returned as if from Ju'athi one evening, loading up oryx-cows balanced between the two saddlebags. We returned with a roan horse led in our midst, the eye moving down across him and then rising. In the morning he was so sleek he would throw our lad — like an arrow smoothed in the hand and slotted. The blood of the lead animals at his chest was like henna-dye pressed into a parted white-haired head.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a herd of wild bulls or aurochs

context: One of the longest and most celebrated hunting qasidas in the Jahili corpus. After the atlal opening and camel-description (rahil), the poem shifts to an extended, vividly detailed hunting expedition — scouts, pursuit, the kill, the feast, and the return. The description of the horse dripping with blood from the hunted animals is among the most striking images in pre-Islamic poetry.

titleEn: Do Not Abandon Me, O Rabi'

verses.sadr: فإمَّا تَرَيْني اليَوْمَ في رأسِ شَاهِقٍ،

translationEn: Do not abandon me, O Rabi', to this fate — I used to consider myself secure in your protection before this. It is a departure contrary to the wish of a prisoner in a village — Arabian villages where they watch the lightning flash. If you see me today on the summit of a high cliff, know that I have ridden out at dawn leading a smooth-coated, eager horse. I have startled unsuspecting wild beasts grazing at peace, and I have unveiled the white-complexioned women of the curtained palanquins, radiant and beautiful. Tender women who reveal from their pure white teeth a fragrance and either a stiff fine garment or anemone-patterned silk.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to hand me over, to abandon me

context: A poem addressed to Rabi', a tribal figure or protector, in which Imru' al-Qays pleads not to be abandoned to his present misfortune. He counters his current destitution with nostalgic recollections of his past life of hunting, horsemanship, and erotic adventure — the three domains that define the aristocratic Jahili self.

titleAr: ألا عِمْ صَبَاحًا أيّهَا الطّلَلُ البَالي
titleEn: Good Morning, O Crumbling Ruin

verses.sadr: ألا عِمْ صَبَاحًا أيّهَا الطّلَلُ البَالي
verses.ajuz: وَهل يَعِمنْ مَن كان في العُصُرِ الخالي

verses.sadr: وَهَل يَعِمَنْ مَن كان أحدثُ عَهدِه
وَتحسِبُ سَلمَى لا تَزَالُ تَرى طَلًا
verses.ajuz: من الوَحشِ أوْ بَيضًا بمَيثاءِ مِحْلالِ
بوَادي الخُزَامى أوْ على رَسّ أوْعالِ
سُمُوَّ حَبَابِ المَاءِ حَالًا على حَالِ
لَناموا فما إنْ من حديثٍ وَلا صَالِ

verses.sadr: وَقد عَلِمَتْ سَلمى وَإنْ كانَ بَعلَها
صرَفتُ الهَوى عنهنّ من خَشيةِ الرَّدى
وَلمْ أسْبَإ الزِّقَّ الرَّوِيَّ وَلمْ أقُلْ
سليمِ الشظى عبلِ الشَّوَى شَنِجِ النَّسا
وَصُمِّ صِلابٌ مَا يَقِينَ مِنَ الوَجَى
verses.ajuz: كأنّ مكانَ الرِّدْفِ مِنهُ على رَالِ
وَكانَ عِدَاءُ الوَحشِ مني على بالِ

verses.sadr: فَلَوْ أنّ ما أسعَى لأدْنى مَعِيشَةٍ
verses.ajuz: كَفاني، وَلمْ أطْلُبْ، قَلِيلٌ مِنَ المَالِ

translationEn: Good morning, O crumbling ruin — and can one who dwelt in a past age still receive a morning greeting? Can any wish well except the blessed and immortal man, little burdened with cares, who does not spend his nights in terror? Can any wish well when the most recent of his memories goes back thirty months across three seasons? These are the abodes of Salma, untended at Dhi Khal, upon which every dark and pouring rainstorm has beaten. You might think Salma has never ceased to see the fawns of the wild and the eggs in the soft, frequented plain. You might think Salma is still as we knew her in the valley of lavender or by the rocky perch of the mountain goats. Those were the nights of Salma when she showed you her towering coiffure and a neck like a gazelle's, adorned with jewels. I gazed at her while the stars were like the lamps of monks lit for the nuns. I rose toward her after her family slept, rising like bubbles of water rippling from surface to surface. She said: 'God has captivated you — you are shaming me! Do you not see the night-talkers and the people around me on all sides?' I said: 'By God, I shall not stir from my seat even if they cut off my head and limbs before you!' I swore to her an impious oath that they would sleep and there would be no more talk or stirring. When we had drawn out the conversation and she was gracious, I bent her — a bough of many branches, swaying. We arrived at the best of it and our words softened, and I tamed what was difficult — what a taming! I woke beloved and her husband woke with a darkened face, evil in his suspicion and thoughts. He snores like a young camel strangled at the throat, trying to kill me — but a man like him is no killer. Will he kill me when the Yemeni sword is my bedmate and blue-tipped, polished spears are like the fangs of ghouls? He has no spear to stab me with, no sword, and is no archer. Will he kill me because I inflamed her heart, as a man who treats a mange-bitten camel inflames it with tar? Salma knows, despite her husband, that a young man raves and is no doer. What wrong has he done me that I remembered slender maidens like gazelles of the sand in the prayer-niches of kings? And a tent of virgins on a cloudy day which I entered — circling with full, rounded elbows, slow with grace. Slender of fingers, noses, and necks — slim of waist — in full and flawless perfection. Tender women who lead desire along the paths of ruin, saying to those of wisdom: he has gone astray, utterly astray. I turned desire away from them for fear of ruin — yet I am not repelled in my friendly qualities, nor one who repels. As if I had never ridden a thoroughbred for pleasure, nor held a full-breasted anklet-wearing girl close. Nor tapped a full wineskin, nor said to my horses: charge again after the rout! Nor attended horses raiding at noon on a powerful, great-bodied, ranging horse. Sound in his knee-joints, thick-shinned, with tensed leg-sinews, with soaring flank muscles above the one who combs him. Hooves hard and solid, never flinching from rough ground, his rump-place as if set upon a high throne. How often I set out at dawn while birds still sat in their nests, with the first rains of al-Wasmi, its scout a lightning flash. The spear-tips swerve away from him — and every dark torrential cloud poured upon him. Riding a strong-flanked, well-muscled she-camel, bay-colored, like the rod of a weaver's loom. I startled with her a herd with clean hides, their lower legs like the pattern of brocade cloaks. The herd of wild bulls, when their running was at its most strenuous, looked on that racing she-camel like horsemen cantering grandly. The herd wheeled and shielded themselves behind a great bull with a long back and curving horn, flat-nosed with a long tail. He ran continuously between bull and cow — and pursuing the wild beasts was ever my purpose. As if I — with a wide-winged, hunting eagle, a she-eagle of the eagles — had swooped my left wing down. Snatching the sand-grouse of the pond at midday, while the mountain foxes had already fenced them off. The hearts of birds, fresh and dried, are at her nest like jujube-berries and withered dates. If what I strive for were the lowest livelihood, a little wealth would suffice me without seeking. But I strive for a deeply-rooted glory — and those like me do attain deeply-rooted glory. A man, while the last breath of life remains in him, cannot reach the ends of great deeds, nor fail to try.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: white gazelle, oryxes used as simile for beauty
to bend down a branch, to embrace forcibly
a Yemeni sword of high quality
the last breath of life, the remnant of a soul

context: A comprehensive qasida containing one of the most famous nocturnal seduction scenes in Arabic poetry — the poet's visit to Salma. The poem moves through multiple registers: the atlal, the memory of the beloved, the erotic encounter, a defiant address to the jealous husband, a description of the women of the tribe, and finally a magnificent wasf al-khayl (horse description) and hunting scene, culminating in a philosophical declaration of the pursuit of glory.

titleAr: دَعْ عَنكَ نَهبًا صِيحَ في حَجَرَاتِهِ
titleEn: Leave Aside the Plunder Cried Out in Its Quarters

verses.sadr: دَعْ عَنكَ نَهبًا صِيحَ في حَجَرَاتِهِ
أبَتْ أجَأٌ أنْ تُسلِمَ العامَ جارَهَا
verses.ajuz: فمنْ شاءَ فَليَنهَضْ لها من مُقاتِلِ

translationEn: Leave aside the plunder cried out in its quarters — but tell me the tale of the traveling camels! It is as if an eagle of the open wasteland — not a lowland eagle — had swooped upon Dithar's milch-camels. Ba'ith toyed with the pledge of Khalid's protection, and 'Isam perished in the earliest calamities. I was amazed by the waddling gait of the short-statured Khalid — like the gait of a she-ass chased away from the watering places. Aja' refused this year to hand over her neighbor to any — so let whoever wants to fight her rise against her. My milch-camels spend the night in safety at al-Quraya, and I graze them on alternate days in the flanks of Ha'il. The sons of Tha'al are their neighbors and protectors, and they are kept safe from the archers of Sa'd and Na'il. Their four-year-olds frolic with the kids of wild ibexes just below the sky on the summits of the high peaks. Crowned and red, with rippling ridgelines — they have wavy markings like patterned striped cloth.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: camels used for travel and riding
an eagle of the vast open desert (strongest kind)
