a short, fat man who walks with a waddling gait
mountain peaks, high towers of rock
ridgelines, the raised lines along a mountain's back

context: A tribal poem touching on themes of alliance, protection, and the security of the poet's camels under the guardianship of Banu Tha'al in the region of Ha'il and Aja'. The poem begins with a dismissal of past grievances and pivots to an account of current circumstances — the mountain topography of the Shammar highlands described with characteristic Jahili precision.

titleEn: O Dwelling of Mawiyya at Ha'il

verses.sadr: وَمِن بَني غُنمِ بنِ دُودَانَ إذْ

translationEn: O dwelling of Mawiyya at Ha'il — on to the flat plain and the two low hollows of 'Aqil! Its echo has gone deaf and its traces have faded, turning away from the speech of the one who questions it. Say to Dudan, slaves of the rod — what emboldened you against the fierce lion? Eyes have now been gladdened with sight of Malik, and of the sons of 'Amr, and of Kahil. And of the sons of Ghunam ibn Dudan, when we cast their highest down upon their lowest. We stabbed them with forward and back thrusts — like two armorers exchanging blows over an arrowhead. When they were crowded together like a swarm of locusts, or like the sand-grouse of Kazima drinking at the water. Until we left them at the battleground — their legs like raised logs of wood. Wine has become lawful to me — I had been a man too occupied to drink it. Today I drink freely, incurring no sin from God and as no gate-crasher.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: slaves of the rod, i.e., lowly cowards
young locusts, a swarm before they can fly
a gate-crasher at a feast, one who drinks without invitation
one who carries something on his back, who bears the consequence
the fierce, indomitable lion (used as metaphor for the poet)

context: A poem of war and vengeance, beginning with the conventional atlal addressed to Mawiyya's abandoned dwelling, then shifting to a boast over the defeat of an enemy clan (Banu Dudan). The poem ends with a striking motif: the poet declares that victory has made wine lawful to him — perhaps a pre-Islamic echo of the idea that the warrior's fulfillment of his duty releases him from self-imposed abstinence.

titleAr: يا لَهْفَ هِنْدٍ إذ خَطِئنَ كاهِلًا
titleEn: Alas, O Hind, That They Missed Kahil

verses.sadr: يا لَهْفَ هِنْدٍ إذ خَطِئنَ كاهِلًا

translationEn: Alas, O Hind, that they missed Kahil — the killers of the king, the mighty chieftain! The best of Ma'add in lineage and generosity, the best of them in character as they well know. We have brought the veteran war-horses in columns — by God, my elder shall not perish in vain! They carry us and the thirsty spear-shafts, and the clan of Sa'b and the tough, quivering spears. Their hooves scattering pebbles as they gallop in file, the rear ranks craning their necks to see the front.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: alas! an exclamation of grief and regret
the ancient tribal confederation of northern Arabs
veteran war-horses in their fifth year and beyond
spear-shafts, also used for a type of rush-grass
thirsty ones — said of spears eager for blood
horses whose tails are tucked under (in battle readiness)

context: A rajaz poem of lament and revenge for the killing of the poet's father, King Hujr. The exclamation 'ya lahf Hind' — 'alas, Hind' — invokes a woman's grief as the emotional center of the vengeance poem. The poem asserts that Kahil, who was missed by the enemies, will be the instrument of retribution.

titleEn: Greet the Departed Camel-Loads by the Side of al-'Azl

verses.sadr: حُلْوٍ، إذا ما جِئْتُ، قالَ: ألا
ما لَمْ أجِدْكَ على هُدَى أثَرٍ

translationEn: Greet the loaded camel-litters by the side of al-'Azl — when their shape is incompatible with mine. What is it that troubles you from a departing caravan, except your youth and your lack of reason? She promised us tomorrow and the day after tomorrow — until she withheld, like the worst of misers. Many a rich, beautiful woman have I cut off from my bond — and I walked on calmly at my own pace. I do not let myself be led to youthful folly by compulsion, nor am I caught by deception. And many a bare, deadly open waste I have crossed with strong, twisted-sinewed she-camels. They spend the night scraping the hard ground with their muzzles, while I spend the night with my elbow on the saddle. With a sharp sword as my pillow, its striking-marks on its flat side like an ant-trail. It is called polished though it has never experienced burnishing or buffing. The dwellings have crumbled — my family is no longer there — and the beautiful suns of cheerful generosity have turned away. She looked at me with a quenched, wide-eyed gaze — large-eyed, bending over her child. She has her necklace and her glance, and over him the nobility of her excellence. I advanced moderately and my forbearance returned to me, and my deeds were set right for piety. God is the most successful of what I seek through Him, and righteousness is the best thing to pack in the saddle-bag. Among the roads there is a crooked one, and guidance is the straight path, and among it is one with hidden rot. I cut off whoever cuts me off, and I renew the bond with whoever seeks to renew it with me. My brother in a faithful, watchful brotherhood — easy in nature and noble in origin. Sweet, who when I come says: 'Welcome — you are in spacious comfort and an easy dwelling!' I shared the morning cup with him, and I did not fail to appreciate the honor of a man's pristine character. I join my rope to your rope, and with the feathers of your arrow I feather my arrow. So long as I do not find you on the track of a straight path that a tracker can follow — tracing your steps before me. My character — you know what it is. And your dogs have never barked at a night-comer like me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: loads carried by camels, the laden caravans
one who moves with dignity and unhurried calm
a she-animal that needs no water, self-sufficient
the morning drink, the dawn cup of wine

context: A long kamil-meter poem displaying unusual thematic range for Imru' al-Qays — moving from the ghazal atlal opening through a self-portrait of stoic dignity in hardship, a sword description, and a remarkable closing section in which the poet praises a brother-friend using the language of tribal solidarity, loyalty, and faith. The verse 'God is the most successful of what I seek through Him' is one of the most quoted religious sentiments in pre-Islamic poetry.

titleEn: O Tha'al! How Far From Me Are the Sons of Tha'al!

verses.sadr: نزَلتُ على عَمرِو بنِ دَرْمَاءَ بُبْطَةً
verses.ajuz: فيا كَرْمَ ما جارٍ وَيَا حُسنَ ما فَعَلِ

translationEn: O Tha'al! How distant from me are the sons of Tha'al! How wonderful — a people who dwell in the mountains! I descended on 'Amr ibn Darma' at Bubta — what a noble neighbor! What good he did! My milch-camel spends her day between Jaww and Mistah, watching over the chick-partridges learning to walk. A group of men kept off predators from her with their bows — protecting her until I said to them: enough! Let the news reach Ma'add and the client-tribes and Tayyi' and Kinda — that I am grateful to the sons of Tha'al.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: those just beginning to walk, newly fledged
enough! a word of sufficiency or satisfaction
the great northern Arab tribal confederation

context: A short poem of gratitude and praise addressed to Banu Tha'al, a subtribe of Tayyi' in the Shammar highlands. Imru' al-Qays stayed as a guest of 'Amr ibn Darma' at a place called Bubta, and his milch-camel was protected by armed men from predators. The poem ends with a public declaration of gratitude sent to all the major tribal confederations — an act of honorable public acknowledgment.

titleEn: I Have Set Down My Saddle Among Banu Tha'al
translationEn: I have set down my saddle among Banu Tha'al — for the noble man is the rightful resting-place of the noble man. I found the best of all people as a neighbor, and the most faithful of them — Abu Hanbal. The closest of them in goodness and the farthest of them in harm — and the most generous of them, who never withholds.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a lodging place, a rightful resting point
the noble, the generous, the honorable
most faithful, most loyal to his word
to be miserly, to withhold from generosity

context: A concise poem of three verses celebrating the poet's host Abu Hanbal of Banu Tha'al. The opening verse contains one of the most quoted sayings in Arabic: 'the noble man is the rightful lodging of the noble man' — a maxim on the mutual attraction of the generous. The poem is a companion piece to the previous ode of gratitude to Banu Tha'al.

titleAr: مَنْ كان يأمُلُ عَقْرَ داريَ مِنْ
titleEn: Whoever Hopes to Destroy My Home

verses.sadr: مَنْ كان يأمُلُ عَقْرَ داريَ مِنْ
يا هَلْ أتَاكَ وَقدْ يُحَدِّثُ ذُو
لَمّا سَمَا مِنْ بَينِ أقْرُنَ فَالـ

translationEn: Whoever among the people of friendship and grudge-bearing hopes to destroy my home — let him come into the midst of my horses with his company, and let him come into the center of my infantry with his army. Has the news reached you? — for an old friend will tell of the shame of treachery. That I, by my life, when I have given my loyalty, have never turned to a substitute or an equal. To a brother with whom I am content, who has shared in kinship, marriage bonds, and excellence. And bonds like the ones I have taken hold of protect one from restlessness and hardship. When he towered above the mountains of Aqrun and the peaks beyond, I said: may my family be his ransom! An ambition that perfection will achieve — that is my opinion of him — he will attain it or exhaust himself trying. He came to the core of Ghatafan and they fell into division — some submitting and some fleeing. He stokes the fire beneath the cooking-pot, kindling it with the wood of the thicket until it boils together.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the destruction of the home, the heart of the camp
the army (named for its five divisions)
something that causes shame, a stigma
a major tribal confederation of central Arabia

context: A poem of self-assertion and warning against enemies who threaten the poet's home, combined with praise for a valued ally. The poem moves between defiance of enemies, loyalty to a brother-friend, and a report of military operations against Ghatafan — reflecting the typical Jahili combination of personal, political, and heroic registers.

titleEn: As If Wine and the Rain's Descent
translationEn: As if wine and the pouring of rain and the scent of lavender and the flowing of honey — the coolness of her front teeth is tinctured with all of these, when the star stands at the center of the sky at midnight.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: lavender, a sweet-smelling Arabian desert plant
to be given a second drink, to be repeatedly tasted
the canine teeth, the upper front teeth

context: A two-verse fragment preserving one of the most celebrated taste-and-scent images in Arabic love poetry. The poet describes the coolness and sweetness of his beloved's teeth by accumulating four perfectly matched sensory comparisons: wine, rain, lavender, and honey. The midnight hour adds to the intimate atmosphere.

titleEn: I Was Exchanged From Wail and Kinda for 'Adwan
translationEn: I was exchanged from Wail and Kinda for 'Adwan and Fahm — be deaf to this, O daughter of the mountain! A people who argue by means of their flocks, and short vultures like the shape of partridges. Tell Shihab and tell 'Asim: has the news come to you of what has happened? We have left among you slain and wounded, and captives like ghoulish hags — walking among our saddle-loads in full acknowledgment, not suffering from hunger or emaciation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to be exchanged, to be replaced (a misfortune)
the great tribal confederation of Bakr and Taghlib
domestic animals, flocks (used as legal proof)
ones who acknowledge their state, who confess

context: A poem of war and boast in the munsarij meter, expressing the poet's indignation at being removed from powerful noble alliances. He mocks the weak tribal confederates foisted upon him, then delivers a stinging war-bulletin to two named enemies: we have killed, wounded, and taken your women captive — and they are not even going hungry in our possession.

titleEn: Did I Not Tell You That Time Is a Ghoul?
translationEn: Did I not tell you that Time is a ghoul — a treacherous covenant-breaker that devours men? It has removed from the fortresses the one of feathers and glory who had possessed the plains and the mountains. A resolute commander who swept across the horizons like a sudden revelation and drove the squadrons toward the east. He built a barrier where the sun rises against Gog and Magog — mountains of stone. So if Shanu'a perishes or changes, travel on — for in Ghassan there is an uncle. By their glory you were made glorious — if they are humbled, then humiliation is what is left to bestow upon you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Time, Fate — the great impersonal force of pre-Islamic thought
a ghoul, a devouring demon of the desert
to scatter completely, to sweep away
Gog and Magog, legendary peoples of the apocalypse
the Ghassanid Arab kingdom allied with Byzantium

context: A philosophical poem on the nature of Time (al-Dahr) as a devouring force that destroys even the mightiest rulers — an allusion likely to Alexander the Great (Dhu al-Qarnayn). The poem ends with a political address advising the listener to seek protection with the Ghassanids after the fall of another dynasty, placing Imru' al-Qays in the context of his own quest for Byzantine support.

titleEn: Your Eyes — Their Tears Overflow in Buckets
translationEn: Your eyes — their tears overflow like buckets, as if their two channels were trickling springs. Or a stream in the shade of palms where the water has room to flow beneath. From the memory of Layla — and where is Layla? — for the best of what I seek is what can be attained. How often I cross the land when it is barren, my companion a full-grown, fast she-camel. Smooth-skinned, with plump neck-veins as if her withers were a smooth hill. As if she were a solitary young wild bull wrapped about by wind and shade. As if she were a she-goat of the valley-bottom galloping when the gazelle has been left alone. A gallop where you see lengths of stride between its bounds, propelled by swift lower legs. And many a low ground I have descended alone — the heart fills with awe at its dark hollows. A tall, swimming-gaited she-camel leads me forward — the grazing and barrenness have kept her lean. She is like a hunting eagle on the chase — her muzzle like a hook raised high. Feeding on the hearts of the sand-grouse of the thicket-frequented land as food, as one feeds dependents. And many a raid with its dust-cloud — its flocks looking like squadrons of cavalry. As if they were scales scattered in the air when the sandals flash in the running. I attacked the tribe at dawn with an early strike — and the men were the most wretched of it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: buckets drawn from a well, copious flow
trickling springs, small scattered water sources
a full-grown camel in its ninth year
a large vein in the neck or foreleg
scales of fish, used as simile for close-packed warriors

context: A qasida in the short mujazza' al-basit meter — a somewhat unusual choice — combining ghazal (memory of Layla, the weeping eyes) with the rahil (the camel journey) and a war boast. The poem contains a chain of similes for the she-camel of unusual variety: a young wild bull, a valley she-goat, a hunting eagle. It ends abruptly with a raid in which the men suffered most.

titleEn: War, When It First Begins, Is a Young Girl
translationEn: War, when it first begins, is a young girl displayed in her adornments to every ignorant fool. Until when it blazes and its flames are kindled, it returns as a crone without a lover. A grey-haired hag who has shorn her head and become unrecognizable — repugnant to smell and kiss.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a blazing fire, the flames of a conflagration
a grey-haired woman, one with mixed black and white hair
she has shaved/shorn her head (sign of mourning and horror)
she has become unrecognizable, she has disguised herself

context: One of the most celebrated three-verse poems in Arabic — a philosophical statement on the deceptive nature of war. It moves from the seductive image of war as a beautified young girl attracting the ignorant, to the horrifying image of war as a shorn, grey crone at its conclusion. This triplet has been quoted for centuries as the definitive Arabic statement on the disillusionment of war, attributed by some scholars also to other poets but most commonly to Imru' al-Qays.

titleEn: A Mail-Clad Warrior Whose Chest I Laid Open with the Spear

verses.sadr: فَجَعتُ بِهِ في مُلتَقَى الحيِّ خَيْلَهُ

translationEn: A mail-clad warrior whose chest I laid open with the spear — I straightened his leaning with a sword of damascened stripes. I bereaved his horses of him at the gathering of the tribe — I left the noble birds hopping around him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one wearing full armor, a fully-mailed warrior
to lay open, to expose, to pierce through
the damascened lines on a sword blade
to bereave, to cause grief by taking away
noble birds — eagles and vultures circling the slain

context: A two-verse fragment of fakhr (self-boasting) describing a single decisive warrior encounter. The poet spears a fully-armored enemy in the chest, then finishes him with a sword stroke. The closing image of noble birds (eagles) hopping around the corpse is a stock Jahili war image but rendered here with particular economy.

titleEn: Whose Are These Dwellings I Passed at Suham?

verses.ajuz: نَبْكي الدّيارَ كما بكى ابنُ خِذامِ
من خَمرِ عانَةَ أوْ كُرُومِ شَبَامِ
وَنُشِدتُ عن حُجرِ ابنِ أمِّ قَطَامِ

verses.sadr: خالي ابنُ كَبشَةَ قد عَلِمَت مكانَهُ

translationEn: Whose are these dwellings I passed at Suham — then 'Amayatayn — then the rocky plateau of Aqdham? Then the smooth rocks of al-Atit — then Sahatayn — then Ghadir, where the wild ewes walk among the white gazelles. A dwelling of Hind and al-Rabab and Fartana and Lamis — before the accidents of the days. Turn aside at the crumbled ruin, for we weep for dwellings as Ibn Khidham wept. Do you not see their camel-loads departing in the morning like date palms of Shawkan at harvest time? Large-eyed women whose skins are perfumed with fragrant powders — white of face, smooth of body. I spent the day in the traces of the dwellings as if I were a man exhilarated by an early morning cup of wine. Fresh wine the color of a gazelle's blood — aged from the vineyards of 'Ana or the vines of Shabam. As if one who drinks it has touched his tongue to a fever that pervades his body with sickness. A stout she-camel I urged forward and she gathered herself into the gait of an ostrich on a hot road. She strides on at ease, her head raised high — startled, her foot-sole worn and bloody. She lurched to throw me and I said to her: 'Stop! I am a man whom throwing is forbidden to you!' So you are rewarded with the best reward of a single camel — and you have returned with your back unharmed and in peace. As if Badr and the connected Kutayfa, and from 'Aqil its ancient ruins. Convey to Subay' if you deliver a message — I am like your worry when I move forward blindly. Limit your threats — for from what I face I cannot even tighten my belt. I am the one who awakens after they have put to sleep, and I am the one who strikes the cheeks of the sleepers. I am the one whose excellence Ma'add has recognized, and I have been asked about Hujr son of Umm Qatam. I engage the hero whose engagement is dreaded — and when I shoot, my arrows do not go wide. My uncle is Ibn Kabsha — you know his station — and Abu Yazid and his clan are my paternal uncles. When I am harmed in a land I bid it farewell — I do not dwell except in a fitting abode.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: camel-loads, loads of the departing caravan; also grudges
large-eyed beautiful women; also the white-eyed gazelle
an early morning cup of old wine
to push a camel forward with a prod or cry
cracked and bloody (of foot pads from hard travel)
the flat of a sword, the cheek; used figuratively for striking
a sword forged in Masharif al-Sham

context: A long kamil qasida of the complex multi-section type, beginning with an atlal for Hind and three other named women, moving through a wine and reverie section, then a camel description (featuring the rare moment when the camel nearly throws the rider), and culminating in a boast section in which Imru' al-Qays asserts his lineage and heroic identity by naming his distinguished maternal and paternal relatives.

titleEn: May God Curse the Barajim Altogether

verses.ajuz: لدى بابِ هندٍ إذ تَجَرّد قائِما

translationEn: May God curse the Barajim altogether, and mutilate Yarbu' and deface Darim! He preferred the taunting of the clan of Mujashi' — necks of slave-women who gather nail-clippings! They did not fight for their lord and his ward, nor did they warn their neighbor so that he might escape safe. And they did not act as al-'Uwayr acted toward his neighbor at Hind's door, when he stood stripped and bare.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the knuckle-joints; also a tribal faction of Tamim
to mutilate by cutting the nose or ears (imprecation)
to rub in the dust, to debase utterly
a place of blame and insult; bitter mockery
a ward, a protégé under a chief's guardianship

context: A poem of hija' (satire and invective) against the Barajim — a faction of the Tamim tribe — for failing in their duty of protection to the poet's father and ward. The final verse contrasts their cowardice with the conduct of al-'Uwayr, a legendary figure who stood exposed at Hind's door to protect his neighbor — the ideal of Jahili male honor and self-sacrifice.

titleEn: As If When I Descended on al-Mu'alla

verses.sadr: أقَرَّ حَشا امرِىء القَيسِ بنِ حُجرٍ

translationEn: It is as if when I descended on al-Mu'alla I descended on the towering peaks of Shamam. Neither the king of Iraq nor the king of Syria has power over al-Mu'alla. He turned back the advance-cloud of Dhu al-Qarnayn until the great king's thundercloud withdrew. The sons of Taym — lights of the darkness — have settled the heart of Imru' al-Qays ibn Hujr.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a famous mountain in the Shammar highlands, emblem of grandeur
the vanguard of a cloud or an army, the advancing front
the Two-Horned One — Alexander the Great in Arabic tradition
a thundercloud rising on the horizon
the heroic, the resolute (an epithet for a great king)
the inner heart, the innermost emotion

context: A four-verse poem in praise of Banu Taym and their protector al-Mu'alla — asserting that even Alexander the Great could not overcome the grandeur of this mountain stronghold. The final verse is notable for the poet naming himself in the third person — a form of self-glorification by detachment found occasionally in Jahili poetry — declaring his heart at peace under the protection of these allies.

titleEn: News Came to Me and My Companions on a Hilltop

verses.ajuz: أبِنْ لي وبَيّن لي الحديثَ المُجمجَمَا

translationEn: News reached me and my companions on the hilltop of Sayila' — a report that chased sleep from me and was painfully true. I said to the man of the 'Ijl tribe, whose home was far: 'Explain to me and make clear this muffled news.' He said: 'May you be saved from curse — 'Amr and Kahil have opened the sanctum of Hujr to violation — it has been left exposed and surrendered.'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a rocky hilltop, a bare rocky elevation
to land heavily (of news), to be fully confirmed
a member of the Banu 'Ijl tribe
a royal greeting formula: 'may you be kept from censure'
the protected sacred enclosure, the inviolable sanctum

context: A three-verse narrative poem recounting the moment the poet received news of his father's murder. He is encamped on a hilltop with his companions; a messenger from the 'Ijl tribe delivers the catastrophic news that 'Amr and Kahil have violated his father Hujr's sanctuary. This poem is considered one of the historically authentic accounts of Imru' al-Qays's reaction to his father's assassination — the moment that, according to tradition, launched his life of wandering.

titleEn: How Have Your Reproaches Found a Path Against Me?
translationEn: How have your reproaches found their way to me, when you never rebuked Hujr or 'Usuma? By the oath of God — nothing shall bring us together again, or our maternal uncles the sons of Jushama — not until the hyenas visit a slaughter so complete it looks like the ruins of Thamud or Iram.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to become established, to find a settled course
the oath of God (a solemn Jahili oath formula)
hyenas — scavengers that mark a battlefield
a great battle, a field of carnage
Thamud and Iram — destroyed peoples of Arabian legend

context: A brief poem of defiance and oath — Imru' al-Qays refuses the reproach of unnamed critics and swears that no reconciliation will come until the enemy is destroyed as utterly as the legendary pre-Islamic peoples Thamud and Iram. The invocation of these destroyed civilizations as the standard of total annihilation is uniquely powerful.

titleEn: When She Saw That the Watering Place Was Her Only Aim

verses.sadr: ألا إنّ قَوْمًا كُنتمُ أمسِ دُونَهُمْ
verses.ajuz: هُمْ مَنعُوا جارًا لكُمْ آلَ غُدْرَانِ

translationEn: When she saw that the watering place was her only aim and the white inner muscles of her legs were bleeding, she headed for the spring near Darij whose shade falls over it and whose surface is covered with waterweed. O Hanzal — had you defended and held firm, I would have praised you with the best and most deserving praise, and you would have satisfied me. Know that a people you were yesterday inferior to — they have protected a neighbor of yours, the clan of Ghudran. 'Uwayr — and who is like 'Uwayr and his clan! And Safwan gladdened in the night of sorrows. The garments of Banu 'Awf are pure and clean, and their faces shine brightly at the great gatherings. They delivered the lost tribe's people to their own kin and escorted them between Iraq and Najran. And now they have become — and God has made them exclusively — the most righteous to their covenant and the most faithful to their neighbors.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a watering place accessible to animals, a ford
the muscles around the chest and flanks that tremble with fear or exertion
pond-scum, waterweed covering a still pool
a subtribe of the Tamim confederation
radiant, shining (of faces at an assembly)
the one who is led astray, the lost one

context: A poem in two distinct sections. The first describes a she-camel reaching a watering hole in desperate need — a set piece of wasf (description). The second is a political statement contrasting the cowardice of the Hanzal clan with the praiseworthy conduct of Banu 'Awf (of Banu 'Uwayr and Banu 'Awf), who protected a neighboring tribe and escorted them to safety. The poem is a complex piece combining nature description, blame, and praise.

titleEn: Whose Ruin Did I See That Stirred My Grief?

verses.sadr: فَإنْ أُمْسِ مَكْرُوبًا فَيَا رُبَّ قَيْنَةٍ
وَإنْ أُمْسِ مَكْرُوبًا فَيَا رُبَّ بُهْمَةٍ
verses.ajuz: كَشَفْتُ إذا مَا اسْوَدّ وَجْهُ الجَبانِ

verses.sadr: وَإنْ أُمْسِ مَكْرُوبًا فَيَا رُبَّ غَارَةٍ
عَلَى رَبِذٍ يَزْدَادُ عَفْوًا إذَا جَرَى

translationEn: Whose ruin is this that I saw and that stirred my grief — like the lines of a scripture on a Yemeni palm-stalk? Dwellings of Hind and al-Rabab and Fartana — our nights at the sandy slope of Badalan! Nights when desire called me and I answered it, and the eyes of those I love gave me full draughts. If I end the day sorrowful — how many a pampered singing-girl have I kept busy with the kiran lute! She has a mizmhar that rises above the army with its sound — deep and resonant when the two hands pluck it. If I end the day sorrowful — how many a valor did I uncover when the coward's face had blackened! If I end the day sorrowful — how many a raid did I witness upon a lean-bellied, loose-chested horse! Upon a swift one that increases in ease as it runs — smoothly flowing, swift in its gallop and high-stepping trot. It strides on solid, hard, rough hooves with tight joints and supple sinews. And many a first spring rain whose high grounds are green — I cut across it on a tall, bare-backed charger. Charging, fleeing, advancing, retreating all at once — like a male gazelle of the hallab plant, fleet and swift. When we took him by the side he would bend his back like a desert-plant stem swaying in the downpour. Enjoy this world, for you shall pass away — from the exhilaration and the beautiful women. The white ones like gazelles and the tawny ones like statues — the chaste among them and the brightly-dressed, the languorous. Is it from the memory of a Nabhani woman whose people settled in the bend of al-Mala that your two eyes rush forward? Their tears are a pouring, a sheeting, a lasting rain — a drizzle, a dripping, and they stream. As if the two eyes were two waterskins of a hurrying traveler — brimful, not yet sealed with grease.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: scripture, written text; also the Psalms
the spine of a palm frond — used as writing surface
a lute-like string instrument, an oud
a plant with pliant, swaying stems; also a soft stone
a large water-skin used for storing water on journeys
brimming full, to the point of overflowing

context: A comprehensive qasida — one of the most formally complete in the Imru' al-Qays corpus — moving through atlal for three named women, lyrical description of past pleasures (music, valor, horsemanship), a horse description containing the famous repeated formula 'mikarrin mifarrin' (also in the Mu'allaqa), a philosophical exhortation to enjoy life, and a closing section of intensely detailed tear imagery — cataloguing six different types of weeping with extraordinary lexical precision.

titleAr: قِفا نَبكِ من ذكرَى حَبيبٍ وَعِرْفانِ
titleEn: Stop — Let Us Weep for a Beloved and Her Trace

verses.sadr: قِفا نَبكِ من ذكرَى حَبيبٍ وَعِرْفانِ

verses.ajuz: كُلىً من شَعِيبٍ ذاتُ سَحٍّ وَتَهْتانِ

verses.sadr: إذا المَرْءُ لم يَخزُنْ عَلَيهِ لِسَانَهُ

verses.ajuz: على ذاتِ لَوْتٍ سَهوَةِ المشْيِ مِذعانِ
أفَانِينَ جَرْيٍ غَيرِ كَزٍّ وَلا وَانِ
كما مَالَ غُصنٌ ناعِمٌ فوْق أغصَانِ

verses.sadr: وَحَتَّى تَرَى الجَونَ الَّذي كانَ بَادِنًا

translationEn: Stop — let us weep for the memory of a beloved and the familiar trace of a dwelling whose signs have faded since long ages past. Years have passed over it after me until it has become like the lines of a scripture in the books of monks. By it I remembered the whole assembled tribe, and it stirred up the lingering traces of sickness from my heart and my sorrows. My tears poured out over my cloak like water-channels pouring and streaming. If a man does not guard his tongue against himself, he is no guard over anything other than it. If you see me carried in the bier of Jabir — on a litter like a winter hut, my death-shrouds flapping — know that how many a desperate man I charged to rescue, and how many a captive I removed the shackle from, and he paid my ransom with prayer. And youths of true quality I roused at dawn — they rose together, some stumbling and some exhilarated. And many a vast expanse whose depths I have cut across upon a she-camel of the swishing tail, smooth of gait, obedient. And many a rain-soaked land colored like the colocynth — I descended into it while every heavy-winged, humming cloud moved over it in succession. On a great horse that gives you varieties of running before you ask — neither cramped nor sluggish. Like the dusty-hued male gazelle for whom an eagle swooped down from the peaks of Thalhan. And many a hollow like the belly of the wild ass — an empty, bewildering waste — I cut across on a handsome horse with a drawn, haggard face. It pushes aside the flanks of the riding beasts with its shoulder, as a supple branch sways above other branches. And many a campaign like the flood-plains of al-Unaim reaching the habitations of the enemy — of great numbers and strongholds. I stretched them out in it until their mounts grew weary, until the thoroughbreds could no longer be led by their reins. Until you see the dark horse that was once fat covered with hovering vultures and eagles. Is this after al-Harith the King, son of 'Amr — whose kingdom reached from Iraq to 'Uman! — dwelling as a neighbor of Banu Shamaja ibn Jurm in what humiliation fate has allotted? And Banu Shamaja ibn Jurm keep their goats away from them — have mercy, O Merciful One!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: recognition, the familiar traces of a known place
lingering traces, the remnants of an illness or sorrow
a continuous pouring rain, an unceasing flow of tears
the iron shackle worn by prisoners
a famous mountain in the Najd plateau, seat of eagles
the depths, the interior distance of a vast desert
birds hovering to feed on carrion — vultures and eagles
have mercy! a supplication to God's mercy

context: A major polythematic qasida whose opening verse — 'stop, let us weep for the memory of a beloved' — is a variant on the most famous opening in Arabic poetry (the Mu'allaqa). The poem moves through extended grief at the atlal, a reflection on death and legacy, praise for the poet's companions, a great horse and desert journey, a war campaign, and concludes with a profoundly melancholy closing — the descendants of a great king (al-Harith ibn 'Amr) reduced to begging mercy from a minor tribe while their goats are kept away from them. The final verse 'hananak dha l-hanan' — O Merciful One, have mercy — is one of the most moving endings in Jahili poetry.

titleAr: أفْسَدتَ بالمَنّ ما أوْلَيتَ من شِعَمٍ
titleEn: Generosity Ruined by Boasting

verses.sadr: أفْسَدتَ بالمَنّ ما أوْلَيتَ من شِعَمٍ

translationEn: You have spoiled with reproach whatever gifts of sustenance you bestowed — the noble man, when he does good, does not then taunt with it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: reproaching someone with a favor given, boasting of generosity
one who reproaches others with his favors, a boastful giver
the noble man, the generous one of character

context: A single-verse epigram on the ethics of generosity. Imru' al-Qays states the pre-Islamic aristocratic ideal: true nobility gives without reminding the recipient of the gift, since taunt (mann) cancels the moral worth of the act entirely.

titleAr: ألا يَا عَينِ! بكّي لي شَنِينَا
titleEn: Weep for the Fallen Kings

verses.sadr: ألا يَا عَينِ! بكّي لي شَنِينَا
مُلُوكًا من بَني حُجْرِ بنِ عَمرٍو

translationEn: O my eye, weep for me copiously — weep for the departing kings! Kings from the sons of Hujr ibn 'Amr, driven away at evening and slain. Would that they had fallen on a day of battle — but they were killed in the homes of the Banu Marin. Their skulls were not washed with any ritual cleansing, but were rolled in blood. The birds hover over them ceaselessly, plucking out their eyebrows and their eyes.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: those who have gone, the departed, the dead
driven, led away like cattle (passive construction)
smeared with dust and blood, rolled in the dirt
hovering, staying persistently in one place
to pluck out, to pull away

context: A lament for the kings of the Kindite royal line, the poet's own kin, who were treacherously killed not in honorable battle but in the homes of the Banu Marin. The lack of proper burial rites — no washing of the skulls — deepens the dishonor. Imru' al-Qays composed elegies for his slaughtered family throughout his life.

titleEn: The Radiant Rudayni Spear
translationEn: I grasped a Rudayni spear whose blade was like the glow of a flame that has not yet mixed with smoke — pure, clean light.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a spear from Rudayna, a famous lance-maker or place known for fine spears
the spearhead, blade of a lance
I gripped, I took hold of, I collected

context: A single-verse fragment describing the dazzling quality of a Rudayni spear, one of the most prized weapons of pre-Islamic Arabia. The simile — flame without smoke — evokes pure radiance untainted by impurity, a mark of the ideal weapon.

titleAr: ومَا هَاجَ هذا الشَّوْقَ غَيْرُ مَنَازِلٍ
titleEn: Ruined Dwellings Stirring Longing

verses.sadr: ومَا هَاجَ هذا الشَّوْقَ غَيْرُ مَنَازِلٍ

verses.ajuz: غَدَتْ في سَوادِ اللّيْلِ قَبْلَ المَثَاني

translationEn: Nothing stirred this longing but crumbling dwellings between Yadhbul and Faraqan. A well-bucket drawn by a tar-smeared she-camel that set out before dawn, moving through the darkness of night before the rope had made its second pass. It is driven by a burly man whose chest and beard are soaked with the spray of splashing water.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to stir up, to provoke, to arouse (an emotion)
a she-camel smeared with tar (used as a draft animal at wells)
the second pass or draw of the well-rope
the chest, breastbone (of a man or animal)
the spray, the splashing of water

context: A short fragment of the atlal tradition in which the poet identifies ruined campsites between two landmarks — Yadhbul and Faraqan — as the trigger for his longing. The poem then shifts to a vivid scene of early-morning water-drawing, a common image of toil and pre-dawn activity in pre-Islamic poetry.

titleAr: ومَا كُنْتُ أخْشَى أنْ أبِيتَ بِحِمْيَرٍ
titleEn: Exile, Gazelles, and Pastoral Plenty

verses.sadr: ومَا كُنْتُ أخْشَى أنْ أبِيتَ بِحِمْيَرٍ
verses.ajuz: غَريبًا ولا أغْدُو إلى بابِ هَمْدَانِ
جَنَى النَّحْلِ غَرْثانًا ولا غَيْرَ غَرْثانِ

verses.sadr: ألا لَيْتَ لي بِالنَّحْلِ أحْيَاءُ عامِلٍ

translationEn: I never feared I would spend the night in Himyar as a stranger, nor set out to the gate of Hamdan. Nor did I expect to wander in Zafar and gather wild honey whether hungry or not. Would that I had in al-Nahl the tribespeople of Amil, and at the Khashilat pools the necks of gazelles! If not camels then let there be goats — the horns of their old she-goats like staffs of wood. Spring blessed them with mountain gazelles and ibex, and the first rains blessed them further. When their milch-goats are milked they low, as if a death-cry had rung out among the clan. They come home at evening as if — from all they have grazed — their udders are hanging water-buckets at their flanks. And they pour out for their household dried curd and clarified butter; and what greater wealth is there than fullness of belly and quenching of thirst!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: stony watering pools, rocky hollows where rainwater collects
the second seasonal rain, coming after the first (وَسْمِيّ)
milch animals, those producing milk (plural of حالبة)
the haunches, the hip area (of an animal)
dried curd cheese, a staple food of the nomadic Arabs

context: A poem of exile and pastoral longing, likely composed during Imru' al-Qays's wanderings in Yemen (Himyar, Hamdan, Zafar are all Yemeni regions). After expressing shock at his displaced condition, the poet shifts into a rich wasf of pastoral abundance — goats fattened by spring rains, milk flowing in abundance, the simple wealth of nomadic life he has lost.
